name: Graph State Machine
summary: Agents are nodes in a directed graph; conditional edges route execution based on shared state.
description: The workflow is defined as a directed graph where nodes represent agents or functions and edges define transitions, including conditional branches that evaluate shared state to determine the next step. A centralized state object persists across the entire graph execution, readable and writable by every node. This lets workflows express loops, branches, parallel fan-out, and human-in-the-loop pauses as explicit graph structure rather than imperative code. The key distinction from a simple pipeline is that control flow is data-dependent -- the same graph can take completely different paths on different inputs.
realWorldExample: LangGraph (LangChain) implements this pattern directly. Agents are graph nodes, edges can be conditional functions that inspect state, and the Send API enables dynamic fan-out to worker nodes. LangGraph's persistence layer can serialize graph state to external storage, enabling pause/resume across sessions. Amazon Bedrock and Google ADK's workflow agents (SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, LoopAgent) implement subsets of this pattern with less flexible graph topologies.
whenToUse: When your agent workflow has non-trivial branching logic, needs to loop conditionally, or requires explicit control over which agent runs next based on intermediate results. Especially valuable when you need persistence, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, or debuggable execution traces.
strengths: Explicit control flow -- you can see and reason about every possible execution path
State persistence enables pause/resume and human-in-the-loop workflows
Conditional edges let the same graph handle diverse inputs with different strategies
Debugging is straightforward -- you can inspect state at every node transition
weaknesses: Graph definition can become complex for highly dynamic workflows
Centralized state creates coupling -- every node reads/writes the same object
Less flexible than fully autonomous agents that decide their own next steps
Framework lock-in -- the graph topology is framework-specific, not portable

agents.name: Graph Orchestrator
agents.description: Manages graph execution, evaluates conditional edges, maintains state, handles persistence and checkpointing

agents.name: Node Agent
agents.description: An LLM-powered or function-based node that reads state, performs work, and writes results back to state

agents.name: Conditional Router
agents.description: A pure function on edges that inspects current state and returns the name of the next node to execute

shared: Graph state object -- the central data structure read and written by all nodes
Checkpoint store -- serialized state snapshots for persistence and rollback
memoryArchitecture.description: All coordination happens through the shared state object. Nodes are stateless functions that transform state. The graph orchestrator manages execution order and persistence. No direct inter-node communication exists outside the state.

diagram:
graph TD
  START[Start] --> A[Agent A]
  A --> CR{Conditional\nRouter}
  CR -->|path 1| B[Agent B]
  CR -->|path 2| C[Agent C]
  B --> D[Agent D]
  C --> D
  D --> CR2{Done?}
  CR2 -->|no| A
  CR2 -->|yes| END[End]
  STATE[(Shared State)] -.->|read/write| A
  STATE -.->|read/write| B
  STATE -.->|read/write| C
  STATE -.->|read/write| D

name: Role-Play Dialogue Pair
summary: Two LLM agents assigned complementary roles converse to solve a task through structured dialogue.
description: Two agents are initialized with complementary role descriptions through inception prompting -- one typically acts as an instructor or user, the other as an assistant or executor. They engage in multi-turn dialogue where the instructor decomposes the task into subtasks and the assistant proposes solutions, with both agents following communication protocols that prevent common failure modes like role flipping, instruction repetition, and infinite loops. The key insight is that having two models talk to each other with structured roles produces more thorough task completion than either model alone, because the instructor keeps pushing for specifics and the assistant keeps generating concrete outputs.
realWorldExample: CAMEL-AI (Communicative Agents for Mind Exploration of Large Language Model Society) pioneered this pattern with inception prompting to guide autonomous agent conversations. ChatDev's chat chain mechanism uses role-play pairs (CEO-CTO, CTO-Programmer, Programmer-Tester) at each phase of software development, with communicative dehallucination to reduce errors. The pattern influenced CrewAI's role-based agent design.
whenToUse: When tasks benefit from iterative refinement through dialogue, when you want to decompose complex work through back-and-forth conversation rather than a single prompt, or when simulating professional interactions (user-developer, teacher-student, interviewer-expert) produces better results.
strengths: Structured roles prevent both agents from converging on a lazy consensus
The instructor agent naturally decomposes tasks into concrete subtasks
Inception prompting creates stable role adherence without fine-tuning
Scales to multi-phase workflows by chaining different role pairs sequentially
weaknesses: Two-agent dialogues can loop or converge prematurely without termination conditions
Role flipping -- agents can lose their assigned perspective mid-conversation
Token-intensive -- multi-turn dialogue uses far more tokens than single-pass approaches
Only two perspectives per conversation, limiting diversity of viewpoints

agents.name: Instructor Agent
agents.description: Assigned the task-decomposition role via inception prompting. Breaks the task into subtasks, evaluates assistant outputs, and pushes for specifics

agents.name: Assistant Agent
agents.description: Assigned the execution role via inception prompting. Proposes solutions, generates code or text, and responds to instructor feedback

shared: Conversation history -- the full multi-turn dialogue accessible to both agents
Task specification -- the original user request that frames the inception prompts
private: Inception prompt -- each agent's role-specific system prompt, invisible to the other
memoryArchitecture.description: Both agents share the conversation history as context. Each agent's inception prompt (system message) defines its role and is private. The dialogue itself is the coordination mechanism -- there is no state outside the conversation.

diagram:
graph LR
  U[User Task] --> IP1[Inception Prompt:\nInstructor Role]
  U --> IP2[Inception Prompt:\nAssistant Role]
  IP1 --> I[Instructor Agent]
  IP2 --> A[Assistant Agent]
  I -->|subtask| A
  A -->|solution| I
  I -->|feedback| A
  A -->|revision| I
  I -->|TASK_DONE| OUT[Final Output]

name: SOP-Driven Software Team
summary: LLM agents simulate a software company, following standard operating procedures through waterfall phases.
description: Agents are assigned corporate roles (product manager, architect, engineer, QA) and follow explicit standard operating procedures (SOPs) that define the workflow. Work proceeds through waterfall phases -- design, coding, testing, documenting -- with structured artifacts passing between phases. Each phase is broken into atomic subtasks handled by role-play pairs. A shared message pool with publish-subscribe routing lets agents communicate without direct coupling. The pattern embodies the insight that human organizational SOPs, when made explicit and machine-readable, are effective coordination mechanisms for LLM agents.
realWorldExample: MetaGPT defines five roles (product manager, architect, project manager, engineer, QA engineer) following SOPs that produce PRDs, system designs, task lists, code, and tests from a one-line requirement, achieving 85.9% Pass@1 on code generation benchmarks. ChatDev simulates a virtual software company with CEO, CTO, CPO, Programmer, Designer, Tester, and Reviewer roles progressing through design-coding-testing phases via chat chains. McKinsey/QuantumBlack's ARK framework uses a rule-based workflow backbone that defines the phase sequence deterministically (requirements, design, code, test) while giving agents within each phase full agentic flexibility -- the SOP is the skeleton, the LLM fills in the muscles.
whenToUse: When the task maps naturally to a well-understood human workflow with defined roles and phases, especially software development. Best when you want structured artifacts at each stage (PRDs, designs, code, tests) rather than a single monolithic output.
strengths: Produces structured intermediate artifacts (PRD, design doc, code, tests) at each phase
SOPs constrain agent behavior, reducing hallucination and off-task drift
Role specialization means each agent only needs domain-relevant context
The waterfall structure creates natural checkpoints for human review
weaknesses: Rigid waterfall phases cannot adapt when requirements change mid-process
Corporate role simulation adds overhead -- agents spend tokens on role-play rather than work
Errors in early phases (bad PRD) cascade through all downstream phases
Does not handle iterative or exploratory work well -- the waterfall only flows one direction

agents.name: Product Manager
agents.description: Analyzes user requirements and produces a product requirements document (PRD) with user stories and competitive analysis

agents.name: Architect
agents.description: Designs system architecture, data structures, and APIs based on the PRD

agents.name: Project Manager
agents.description: Breaks the design into implementation tasks and assigns them to engineers

agents.name: Engineer
agents.description: Implements code based on task assignments, architecture docs, and API specifications

agents.name: QA Engineer
agents.description: Tests code against requirements, reports bugs, and verifies fixes

shared: Artifact chain -- PRD, design doc, task list, code, test results passed between phases
Message pool -- publish-subscribe system for cross-role communication
private: Role-specific SOP -- each agent's standard operating procedure for its phase
memoryArchitecture.description: Coordination happens through structured artifacts passed between phases. The message pool provides a shared communication channel, but most information flows sequentially through the artifact pipeline. Each agent's SOP is private and defines its behavior.

diagram:
graph TD
  REQ[User Requirement] --> PM[Product Manager]
  PM -->|PRD| ARCH[Architect]
  ARCH -->|Design Doc| PJM[Project Manager]
  PJM -->|Task List| ENG[Engineers]
  ENG -->|Code| QA[QA Engineer]
  QA -->|Bug Reports| ENG
  QA -->|Approved| OUT[Final Software]
  MP[(Message Pool)] -.-> PM
  MP -.-> ARCH
  MP -.-> PJM
  MP -.-> ENG
  MP -.-> QA

name: Conversational Group Chat
summary: Multiple agents solve problems through a shared conversation thread managed by a speaker-selection mechanism.
description: Multiple named agents with distinct roles participate in a shared conversation thread. A chat manager determines which agent speaks next, using round-robin, LLM-based selection, or custom logic. Agents can see the full conversation history and build on each other's contributions. The pattern is conversational rather than task-based -- agents collaborate through discussion, debate, and refinement rather than through structured handoffs or pipelines. The chat manager is the key coordination mechanism, preventing chaos by controlling turn-taking while allowing flexible, emergent collaboration.
realWorldExample: Microsoft's Agent Framework merges AutoGen and Semantic Kernel into a unified platform used by 10,000+ organizations, with group chat as the primary coordination pattern. AutoGen 0.4 redesigned with a layered architecture where agents communicate through a shared message bus with a GroupChatManager selecting speakers. Semantic Kernel's GroupChat orchestration implements the same pattern with a unified API, and the merger means both frameworks now share the same agent runtime. AG2 (the community fork of AutoGen v0.2) maintains backward-compatible group chat functionality.
whenToUse: When the problem benefits from multiple perspectives in free-form discussion, when you need agents to build on each other's ideas iteratively, or when the optimal task decomposition is not known in advance and should emerge from conversation.
strengths: Flexible -- agents can organically shift focus based on conversation dynamics
Builds on each other's work naturally through conversational context
Easy to add or remove agents from the group without restructuring
Speaker selection can be tuned from fully autonomous to tightly controlled
weaknesses: Context window pressure -- every agent sees the full conversation history
Speaker selection is a hard problem -- wrong choices waste tokens on irrelevant contributions
No structured output guarantee -- conversations can meander without producing concrete results
Difficult to debug -- emergent behavior from multi-agent conversation is hard to predict

agents.name: Chat Manager
agents.description: Controls turn-taking by selecting which agent speaks next. Can use round-robin, LLM-based selection, or rule-based logic

agents.name: Specialist Agent
agents.description: A domain-specific agent (coder, reviewer, planner, researcher) that contributes expertise to the group discussion

agents.name: User Proxy
agents.description: Represents the human user in the conversation, can execute code or relay human feedback

shared: Conversation history -- the complete group chat thread visible to all agents
Speaker selection state -- who has spoken, who is queued, selection policy
private: Agent system prompts -- each agent's role definition and expertise description
memoryArchitecture.description: The conversation thread IS the shared memory. All agents read the full history before contributing. The chat manager maintains metadata about speaker order and selection policy. No external state beyond the conversation.

diagram:
graph TD
  CM[Chat Manager] -->|select speaker| A1[Coder]
  CM -->|select speaker| A2[Reviewer]
  CM -->|select speaker| A3[Planner]
  CM -->|select speaker| UP[User Proxy]
  A1 -->|message| GT[Group Thread]
  A2 -->|message| GT
  A3 -->|message| GT
  UP -->|message| GT
  GT -->|history| CM
  GT -->|context| A1
  GT -->|context| A2
  GT -->|context| A3

name: Supervisor-Router Hierarchy
summary: A supervisor agent analyzes requests and routes them to specialized sub-agents, with smart fallback to full orchestration.
description: A supervisor agent sits at the top, receiving all requests. For simple requests with clear intent, it routes directly to the appropriate specialist sub-agent (fast path). For complex or ambiguous requests, it falls back to full orchestration mode -- analyzing the problem, decomposing it into subtasks, coordinating multiple sub-agents, and synthesizing results. The supervisor maintains a registry of sub-agent capabilities and can invoke them in parallel. This dual-mode operation (fast routing vs. full orchestration) is the key distinction from a simple hub-and-spoke -- the supervisor adapts its coordination depth to task complexity.
realWorldExample: Amazon Bedrock's multi-agent collaboration provides supervisor mode and supervisor-with-routing mode, where simple requests bypass full orchestration and route directly to specialized sub-agents. Anthropic's multi-agent research system uses a lead agent that spawns parallel subagents for different aspects of a query. Salesforce Agentforce uses the Atlas reasoning engine as a supervisor that classifies intent, selects topics, and orchestrates domain-specific agent actions across CRM workflows. Klarna built their customer service agents on LangGraph with a supervisor coordinating routing across refund, order-tracking, and FAQ specialists. Bertelsmann uses a coordinator agent that delegates to domain-specific agents across their media divisions, each with independent tool access. Exa's research system uses a Planner-Tasks-Observer triad where the planner decomposes queries, task agents execute search in parallel, and an observer synthesizes and decides whether to iterate. Komodor's Klaudia uses an incident commander agent that dispatches across 50+ specialist agents for Kubernetes troubleshooting, each mapped to a specific failure domain.
whenToUse: When you have a diverse set of specialized agents and need intelligent request routing. Especially valuable in enterprise settings where most requests are routine (fast path) but some require multi-step orchestration (full path).
strengths: Dual-mode operation handles both simple and complex requests efficiently
Sub-agents can be developed, tested, and deployed independently
The supervisor provides a single entry point that simplifies the external API
Parallel sub-agent invocation reduces latency for multi-part tasks
weaknesses: The supervisor is a single point of failure -- if it misroutes, everything fails
Routing accuracy degrades with large numbers of similar sub-agents
Full orchestration mode is expensive -- the supervisor uses significant tokens planning and evaluating
Sub-agent capabilities must be described precisely or the supervisor cannot route correctly

agents.name: Supervisor
agents.description: Receives all requests, classifies intent, routes simple requests to specialists, and orchestrates complex multi-step tasks by decomposing and coordinating

agents.name: Specialist Sub-Agent
agents.description: Handles a specific domain (product recommendations, order tracking, technical support, etc.) with focused tools and knowledge

agents.name: Fallback Agent
agents.description: Handles requests that no specialist can address, escalates to human operators when necessary

shared: Capability registry -- descriptions of each sub-agent's domain and tools
Routing history -- log of past routing decisions for analysis and improvement
private: Specialist knowledge -- each sub-agent's domain-specific context and tools
Conversation state -- per-session context maintained by each specialist
memoryArchitecture.description: The supervisor maintains a registry of sub-agent capabilities. Each specialist maintains its own conversation context across interactions. Routing history enables offline analysis of routing accuracy.

diagram:
graph TD
  REQ[Request] --> SUP[Supervisor]
  SUP -->|simple: route| S1[Specialist A]
  SUP -->|simple: route| S2[Specialist B]
  SUP -->|simple: route| S3[Specialist C]
  SUP -->|complex: orchestrate| PLAN[Plan & Decompose]
  PLAN -->|subtask 1| S1
  PLAN -->|subtask 2| S2
  S1 -->|result| AGG[Aggregate]
  S2 -->|result| AGG
  S3 -->|result| SUP
  AGG -->|synthesis| SUP
  SUP -->|unknown| FB[Fallback]
  SUP --> RESP[Response]

name: Agent-as-Tool Orchestration
summary: Sub-agents are exposed as callable tools to an orchestrator, which invokes them like functions.
description: Instead of agents communicating through messages or shared state, sub-agents are wrapped as tools (functions) that the orchestrator can call. The orchestrator is itself an LLM agent that decides which tool-agent to invoke based on the current task, passes arguments, and receives structured results. This leverages the native tool-use/function-calling capabilities of modern LLMs, making agent coordination feel like programming rather than messaging. Sub-agents are black boxes to the orchestrator -- it knows their capabilities from their descriptions but not their internal implementation.
realWorldExample: LlamaIndex's AgentWorkflow exposes sub-agents as callable tools to an orchestrator agent, with built-in streaming and state management. CrewAI Flows wraps Crews (agent teams) as callable units within larger workflows. Google ADK allows LlmAgent transfer where sub-agents are invoked as tools with structured inputs and outputs. OpenAI's function-calling API is the primitive that enables this pattern.
whenToUse: When you want composable agent systems where sub-agents can be developed, tested, and versioned independently. When the orchestrator benefits from treating sub-agents as black-box capabilities rather than conversation partners.
strengths: Composable -- sub-agents can be mixed, matched, and reused across different orchestrators
Clean interface -- tool descriptions define the contract between orchestrator and sub-agents
Leverages native LLM function-calling, which is well-optimized in modern models
Sub-agents can be heterogeneous -- different models, different frameworks, even non-LLM services
weaknesses: No inter-agent communication -- sub-agents cannot build on each other's work directly
Orchestrator must understand all tool capabilities to choose correctly
Structured input/output constrains the richness of agent interaction
Nested tool calls (agent calling agent calling agent) can create latency chains

agents.name: Orchestrator
agents.description: An LLM agent with access to sub-agents as tools. Decides which to invoke, passes structured inputs, and synthesizes results

agents.name: Tool Agent
agents.description: A sub-agent wrapped as a callable tool with a name, description, and structured input/output schema

shared: Tool registry -- descriptions and schemas for all available tool-agents
Call history -- log of tool invocations, inputs, and outputs
private: Orchestrator planning state -- the orchestrator's reasoning about which tools to call
Tool-agent internal state -- each sub-agent's context, invisible to the orchestrator
memoryArchitecture.description: The tool registry is the only shared knowledge. Tool-agents are stateless from the orchestrator's perspective -- each call is independent. The orchestrator maintains its own planning state across multiple tool calls within a session.

diagram:
graph TD
  USER[User Request] --> ORCH[Orchestrator Agent]
  ORCH -->|function call| T1[Research Agent\nas Tool]
  ORCH -->|function call| T2[Code Agent\nas Tool]
  ORCH -->|function call| T3[Review Agent\nas Tool]
  ORCH -->|function call| T4[Deploy Agent\nas Tool]
  T1 -->|structured result| ORCH
  T2 -->|structured result| ORCH
  T3 -->|structured result| ORCH
  T4 -->|structured result| ORCH
  ORCH --> RESP[Response]
  TR[(Tool Registry)] -.->|descriptions| ORCH

name: Skill Library Curriculum
summary: An agent builds a retrievable library of executable skills, using curriculum-driven exploration to expand capabilities.
description: An automatic curriculum proposes increasingly challenging tasks. For each task, the agent retrieves relevant existing skills from a library indexed by semantic embedding, attempts to compose them or write new code to solve the task, then verifies success through environment feedback. Successful solutions are added to the skill library as reusable, composable programs. Failed attempts trigger iterative refinement using environment feedback and error messages. Over time, the library compounds -- complex skills are built by composing simpler ones, and the curriculum naturally progresses to harder challenges as easier ones are solved. This is lifelong learning for LLM agents.
realWorldExample: Voyager (NVIDIA/MineDojo) is the first LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent in Minecraft. It uses GPT-4 to write JavaScript skills, stores them in a vector-indexed library, and retrieves top-5 relevant skills when facing new tasks. Voyager obtains 3.3x more unique items and unlocks tech tree milestones up to 15.3x faster than prior methods. The skill library transfers to new worlds, solving novel tasks from scratch using accumulated knowledge.
whenToUse: When agents operate in open-ended environments where the full task space is not known in advance, when skills can be composed to handle novel situations, or when you want agents that improve over time without human curriculum design.
strengths: Lifelong learning -- the agent genuinely improves over time as the skill library grows
Compositionality -- complex skills are built from simpler ones, compounding capability
Transferable -- the skill library can bootstrap new agent instances in new environments
Avoids catastrophic forgetting -- skills are stored as code, not as model weights
weaknesses: Requires a verifiable environment -- the agent needs clear success/failure signals
Skill library quality depends on verification -- unverified skills can poison the library
Curriculum generation is itself a hard problem -- too easy and progress stalls, too hard and the agent fails
Code-based skills are brittle to environment changes (API updates, world changes)

agents.name: Curriculum Agent
agents.description: Proposes the next task based on current capabilities and exploration progress, ensuring challenges match the agent's skill level

agents.name: Skill Coder
agents.description: Retrieves relevant existing skills, writes new code to solve the current task, and iteratively refines based on environment feedback

agents.name: Skill Verifier
agents.description: Executes generated code in the environment, checks for success, and reports errors back for refinement

agents.name: Skill Librarian
agents.description: Maintains the skill library -- indexes new skills by semantic embedding, handles retrieval queries, prunes low-quality entries

shared: Skill library -- vector-indexed collection of executable code with semantic descriptions
Exploration log -- which tasks have been attempted, succeeded, or failed
private: Curriculum state -- the agent's model of what skills are missing and what to try next
Iterative refinement context -- error messages and feedback from the current attempt
memoryArchitecture.description: The skill library is the central persistent artifact. It is indexed by embedding for semantic retrieval and grows monotonically with successful task completions. The curriculum maintains a map of explored vs. unexplored capabilities.

diagram:
graph TD
  CURR[Curriculum Agent] -->|next task| SC[Skill Coder]
  LIB[(Skill Library)] -->|top-5 relevant skills| SC
  SC -->|new code| VER[Skill Verifier]
  VER -->|execute in env| ENV[Environment]
  ENV -->|feedback + errors| VER
  VER -->|success| LIB
  VER -->|failure| SC
  SC -->|refined code| VER
  LIB -->|capabilities| CURR

name: Plan-Code-Verify Loop
summary: An autonomous coding agent plans changes, writes code, runs tests, and loops until verification passes.
description: The agent operates in three distinct phases that loop until completion. In plan mode, it analyzes the task, maps out the codebase, and produces an ordered list of changes. In code mode, it writes or modifies files, installs dependencies, and sets up infrastructure. In verify mode, it runs the code, executes tests, checks for errors, and evaluates whether the task is complete. If verification fails, the agent loops back with error context to plan a fix. Human checkpoints gate the transition from planning to execution and from execution to pull request, providing guardrails without micromanagement. The pattern reflects how production coding agents actually work -- not as single-shot code generators but as iterative plan-test-fix loops.
realWorldExample: Stripe's Minions system produces 1000+ PRs per week using a blueprint architecture where a planner generates a structured change plan, coders implement it, and verifiers run CI/lint/tests in a loop until all checks pass. Replit Agent v3 runs autonomous 200-minute sessions with a plan-execute-verify cycle, spawning manager, editor, and verifier sub-agents that iterate until tests pass or the session budget is exhausted. Devin (Cognition) operates similarly with planning and PR checkpoints requiring human approval. GitHub Copilot's agent mode reads files, runs code, checks output, identifies errors, and loops back to fix them autonomously.
whenToUse: When building autonomous coding agents that need to handle complete features or projects, not just single-file edits. The plan-verify loop is essential when the agent must handle its own errors rather than relying on human debugging.
strengths: Self-correcting -- errors from verification feed back into the next plan-code cycle
Human checkpoints provide safety without blocking autonomous work
Produces working, tested code rather than untested suggestions
The plan phase prevents thrashing on complex multi-file changes
weaknesses: Can enter infinite fix loops on subtle bugs the LLM cannot diagnose
Planning accuracy limits overall effectiveness -- bad plans waste many cycles
Verification requires a runnable environment (sandbox, CI, tests) which adds infrastructure
Token-intensive -- each plan-code-verify cycle consumes significant context

agents.name: Planner
agents.description: Analyzes the task and codebase, produces an ordered plan of changes. Replans after verification failures with error context

agents.name: Coder
agents.description: Implements the plan by writing code, modifying files, installing dependencies, and configuring infrastructure

agents.name: Verifier
agents.description: Runs code, executes tests, checks for errors, lint violations, and type issues. Reports pass/fail with diagnostic context

agents.name: Human Gate
agents.description: Reviews plans before execution and pull requests before merging. Provides feedback that triggers replanning

shared: Plan document -- the ordered list of changes, updated after each verification failure
Verification log -- test results, error messages, and diagnostic context from each cycle
private: Codebase index -- the coder's semantic understanding of the project structure
Human feedback -- accumulated reviewer preferences and constraints
memoryArchitecture.description: The plan document is the primary coordination artifact. It is created by the planner, consumed by the coder, and updated with error context from the verifier. The verification log accumulates across cycles, building a history of what has been tried.

diagram:
graph TD
  TASK[Task / Issue] --> PLAN[Plan Mode]
  PLAN -->|ordered changes| HG1{Human\nApproval?}
  HG1 -->|approved| CODE[Code Mode]
  HG1 -->|feedback| PLAN
  CODE -->|modified files| VERIFY[Verify Mode]
  VERIFY -->|tests pass| HG2{Human\nReview?}
  VERIFY -->|tests fail| PLAN
  HG2 -->|approved| PR[Pull Request]
  HG2 -->|changes requested| PLAN

name: Generative Agent Society
summary: LLM agents with persistent memory inhabit a shared environment, forming relationships and coordinating through emergent social dynamics.
description: Each agent maintains a complete record of its experiences as natural language observations, periodically synthesizes these into higher-level reflections, and retrieves relevant memories dynamically when planning behavior. Agents inhabit a shared simulated environment where they can observe each other, form relationships, spread information, and coordinate activities -- all emergently, without top-down orchestration. The architecture has three core components: observation (perceiving the environment and other agents), reflection (synthesizing observations into higher-level insights), and planning (generating behavior based on retrieved memories and current context). Social dynamics emerge naturally -- agents form friendships, organize events, spread gossip, and develop daily routines.
realWorldExample: Stanford's Generative Agents paper (Park et al.) instantiated 25 agents in a Sims-inspired sandbox town, where they autonomously formed relationships, organized a Valentine's Day party through emergent coordination, and maintained consistent personalities over days of simulation. AgentVerse extends this with dynamic group formation and a four-stage process (recruitment, collaboration, execution, evaluation). AgentSociety scales to 10,000+ agents simulating 5 million interactions.
whenToUse: When you want to study emergent social behavior, simulate population-level dynamics, test how information spreads through communities, or create believable NPC behavior in games and virtual worlds.
strengths: Emergent coordination without any top-down orchestration or predefined workflows
Persistent memory enables consistent long-term personalities and relationships
Reflection mechanism prevents memory overload by synthesizing raw observations into insights
Highly realistic social dynamics emerge from simple architectural components
weaknesses: Extremely token-intensive -- each agent's memory retrieval and reflection consumes significant compute
Scaling is expensive -- cost grows multiplicatively with agent count and simulation length
No task guarantee -- emergent behavior is interesting but not goal-directed
Memory retrieval quality degrades as observation count grows without careful curation

agents.name: Generative Agent
agents.description: An autonomous agent with persistent observation memory, reflection capability, and planning behavior. Forms relationships, makes decisions, and acts in the shared environment

agents.name: Environment Engine
agents.description: Manages the shared world state, processes agent actions, generates observations, and enforces physical constraints

shared: Environment state -- the current state of the shared world (locations, objects, time)
Public observations -- what agents can see about each other's actions
private: Observation stream -- timestamped natural language records of everything the agent has perceived
Reflections -- higher-level synthesized insights derived from observations
Plans -- the agent's current intended actions and schedule
memoryArchitecture.description: Each agent maintains a private memory stream of observations and reflections. The reflection mechanism periodically synthesizes recent observations into higher-level insights (e.g., 'I have been talking to Alice a lot lately, we are becoming friends'). Memory retrieval uses recency, importance, and relevance scoring to pull the most useful memories for each decision.

diagram:
graph TD
  ENV[Shared Environment] -->|observations| A1[Agent 1]
  ENV -->|observations| A2[Agent 2]
  ENV -->|observations| A3[Agent N]
  A1 -->|actions| ENV
  A2 -->|actions| ENV
  A3 -->|actions| ENV
  subgraph Agent Memory
    OBS[Observations] --> REF[Reflections]
    REF --> PLAN[Planning]
    PLAN --> ACT[Action]
  end
  A1 -.->|sees| A2
  A2 -.->|sees| A3
  A3 -.->|sees| A1

name: Deterministic-Agentic Hybrid Pipeline
summary: Interleaves deterministic code nodes (git, lint, CI) with agentic LLM nodes in a fixed execution graph.
description: The workflow is a fixed directed graph where some nodes are deterministic programs (git operations, linters, test runners, CI checks) and others are agentic LLM nodes (planning, code generation, error diagnosis). The graph topology is defined ahead of time, not decided by the LLM at runtime. Deterministic nodes provide reliable, repeatable operations that ground the pipeline in reality -- they cannot hallucinate. Agentic nodes provide the flexibility to handle ambiguity, generate novel code, and reason about errors. The key distinction from a pure graph-state-machine (where an LLM routes between LLM nodes) is that the deterministic nodes enforce hard constraints that the LLM cannot bypass or reason around. And the distinction from sop-driven-software-team is that there are no simulated human roles -- just a pipeline mixing code and intelligence.
realWorldExample: Stripe's Minions system uses a blueprint architecture where a planner LLM generates a structured change plan, then deterministic nodes run git checkout, apply file edits, execute linters, and trigger CI -- with the LLM only re-engaging when a deterministic step fails and needs diagnosis. This hybrid approach produces 1000+ PRs per week at Stripe, with the deterministic nodes providing the reliability guarantees that let the system operate at scale without human review of every intermediate step.
whenToUse: When your agent pipeline includes steps that must be deterministic (CI checks, linting, git operations, API calls with exact schemas) alongside steps that require LLM reasoning. Especially valuable when you need auditability -- deterministic nodes produce predictable, reproducible outputs that are easy to log and verify.
strengths: Deterministic nodes cannot hallucinate, providing hard reliability anchors in the pipeline
The fixed graph topology makes execution predictable and debuggable
LLM nodes only engage where flexibility is genuinely needed, reducing cost and latency
Deterministic checks (lint, test, CI) provide objective pass/fail signals that prevent error propagation
weaknesses: The fixed graph cannot adapt its topology to novel situations -- only the LLM nodes are flexible
Designing the right mix of deterministic and agentic nodes requires upfront architectural knowledge
Deterministic nodes create hard failure points -- if CI is misconfigured, the whole pipeline blocks
The LLM must understand the deterministic nodes' interfaces precisely or produce incompatible outputs

agents.name: Blueprint Planner
agents.description: An LLM agent that analyzes the task and produces a structured change plan consumed by downstream deterministic and agentic nodes

agents.name: Agentic Coder
agents.description: An LLM agent that generates or modifies code based on the plan, operating between deterministic validation steps

agents.name: Error Diagnostician
agents.description: An LLM agent that engages only when a deterministic node fails, analyzing error output and proposing fixes

agents.name: Deterministic Node
agents.description: A non-LLM program node (git, linter, test runner, CI) that executes a fixed operation and returns structured pass/fail results

shared: Blueprint document -- the structured change plan produced by the planner and consumed by all nodes
Pipeline execution log -- the pass/fail results from every deterministic and agentic node
private: Planner's codebase index -- semantic understanding of the project used to generate the blueprint
Error diagnosis context -- the diagnostician's analysis of deterministic node failures
memoryArchitecture.description: The blueprint is the primary coordination artifact, created by the planner and consumed by all downstream nodes. Deterministic nodes produce structured results (pass/fail with error output) that feed back into agentic nodes when diagnosis is needed. The pipeline execution log provides full auditability.

diagram:
graph TD
  TASK[Task] --> PLAN[Blueprint Planner\nLLM]
  PLAN -->|blueprint| GIT[Git Checkout\nDeterministic]
  GIT --> CODE[Agentic Coder\nLLM]
  CODE -->|files| LINT[Linter\nDeterministic]
  LINT -->|pass| TEST[Test Runner\nDeterministic]
  LINT -->|fail| DIAG[Diagnostician\nLLM]
  DIAG --> CODE
  TEST -->|pass| CI[CI Pipeline\nDeterministic]
  TEST -->|fail| DIAG
  CI -->|pass| PR[Pull Request]
  CI -->|fail| DIAG

name: Single-Agent Context Maximizer
summary: A single agent with aggressive context compression outperforms multi-agent coordination by eliminating handoff losses.
description: Instead of splitting work across multiple agents, a single agent handles the entire task with sophisticated context management: aggressive compression of irrelevant context, structured memory files for long-term state, and tool-use for operations that would otherwise consume context. The core argument is that multi-agent coordination introduces a coordination tax -- agents make implicit decisions while acting, and when multiple agents make conflicting implicit decisions, the results are worse than a single agent making all decisions coherently. This is explicitly an anti-pattern to multi-agent orchestration, arguing that the overhead of agent handoffs, context serialization, and decision reconciliation exceeds the benefits of parallelism for most coding tasks.
realWorldExample: Cognition's Devin team explicitly argues against multi-agent architectures for coding, stating that 'actions carry implicit decisions, and conflicting decisions carry bad results.' Their single-agent approach uses context compression and structured tool-use to handle tasks that competitors split across multiple agents. The Devin architecture demonstrates that a single agent maintaining full decision coherence, augmented with aggressive context management, can outperform multi-agent systems on complex software engineering tasks where decisions are deeply interdependent.
whenToUse: When tasks involve deeply interdependent decisions where splitting across agents would create coordination overhead that exceeds parallelism benefits. When decision coherence matters more than throughput. When the task is primarily sequential and the agent's context window (with compression) can hold the relevant state.
strengths: Zero coordination overhead -- no handoff protocols, no context serialization, no decision reconciliation
Full decision coherence -- every action is informed by every previous decision with no information loss
Simpler architecture -- one agent, one context, one set of tools, easier to debug and monitor
No conflicting implicit decisions -- a single agent cannot disagree with itself across parallel branches
weaknesses: Context window is still finite -- aggressive compression helps but information is still lost
Cannot parallelize truly independent subtasks, limiting throughput on parallelizable workloads
Single point of failure -- if the agent gets confused, there is no fresh perspective to recover
Scales poorly to tasks that genuinely require diverse expertise or operate across multiple codebases simultaneously

agents.name: Sole Agent
agents.description: The single agent handling all aspects of the task -- planning, coding, testing, debugging -- with full context coherence

agents.name: Context Manager
agents.description: An internal mechanism (not a separate LLM) that compresses, indexes, and manages the agent's context to maximize useful information density

private: Compressed context -- the agent's aggressively compressed view of the full task state
Structured memory files -- long-term state stored in files rather than context window
Decision log -- explicit record of decisions made to prevent re-deriving them after compression
memoryArchitecture.description: All state is private to the single agent. Context compression is the key mechanism -- the agent periodically distills its context, moving low-priority information to structured files retrievable by tool-use. The decision log prevents the agent from losing track of decisions that were compressed away. There is no shared state because there is only one agent.

diagram:
graph TD
  TASK[Task] --> SA[Sole Agent]
  SA -->|plan| SA
  SA -->|code| SA
  SA -->|test| SA
  SA -->|debug| SA
  SA <-->|compress/retrieve| CM[Context Manager]
  CM <-->|read/write| MF[Memory Files]
  SA -->|tool use| TOOLS[Tools]
  TOOLS -->|results| SA
  SA --> OUTPUT[Complete Output]

name: Self-Learning Memory Loop
summary: Agents operate across three knowledge layers -- static truth, queryable knowledge, and auto-captured experiential memory -- improving with each incident.
description: The system organizes knowledge into three distinct layers with different update frequencies. The Blueprint layer contains static, human-curated truth (architecture docs, runbooks, configuration schemas) that changes only through explicit human updates. The Knowledge Base layer contains queryable, structured information (past incidents, system topology, dependency maps) that agents consult on demand. The Self-Learning Memory layer is the novel element: agents automatically capture root causes, successful remediation paths, and failure signatures from each incident, storing them as retrievable memories. On the next similar incident, the agent retrieves these memories and applies learned solutions without retraining. The system improves monotonically with each incident -- the more it encounters, the better it gets, with no human curation needed for the experiential layer.
realWorldExample: Komodor's Klaudia Kubernetes troubleshooting system implements three knowledge layers: Blueprint (cluster architecture, Helm charts, GitOps configs as static ground truth), Knowledge Base (queried on demand for historical context), and Self-Learning Memory (auto-captured root causes from resolved incidents). When Klaudia's 50+ specialist agents resolve an incident, the root cause and remediation path are automatically indexed into the self-learning layer. The next time a similar symptom appears, the incident commander agent retrieves the previous resolution and applies it directly, reducing mean-time-to-resolution with each incident without any model retraining.
whenToUse: When agents operate in environments with recurring patterns (incidents, support tickets, operational issues) and you want the system to improve over time without retraining. When you need to separate immutable truth from learned experience. When operational knowledge is too dynamic for static runbooks but too valuable to discard after each incident.
strengths: Improves monotonically with each incident -- the more the system encounters, the better it gets
No retraining needed -- experiential memory is stored as retrievable text, not model weights
Three-layer separation prevents learned heuristics from corrupting authoritative ground truth
Auto-capture requires no human curation for the experiential layer, reducing maintenance burden
weaknesses: Experiential memory can accumulate incorrect root cause attributions that propagate to future incidents
Retrieval quality depends on similarity matching -- novel incidents may retrieve misleading past experiences
The self-learning layer grows unboundedly and may need periodic curation to prevent noise accumulation
Over-reliance on past patterns can cause the system to miss novel failure modes that resemble past ones

agents.name: Incident Commander
agents.description: Triages incoming incidents, queries all three knowledge layers, dispatches specialist agents, and synthesizes the resolution

agents.name: Domain Specialist
agents.description: Handles a specific failure domain (networking, storage, scheduling, etc.) with focused tools and domain expertise

agents.name: Memory Writer
agents.description: After incident resolution, extracts the root cause, remediation path, and symptom signature and writes them to the self-learning memory layer

agents.name: Memory Retriever
agents.description: Given an incident's symptoms, searches the self-learning memory layer for similar past incidents and returns relevant root causes and remediations

shared: Blueprint layer -- static, human-curated ground truth (architecture docs, runbooks, schemas)
Knowledge base -- structured, queryable information (past incidents, topology, dependencies)
Self-learning memory -- auto-captured root causes, remediation paths, and symptom signatures from resolved incidents
private: Specialist domain context -- each specialist's focused tools and domain knowledge
Incident working state -- the current investigation's hypotheses, evidence, and ruling-out log
memoryArchitecture.description: Three knowledge layers with different update frequencies. The blueprint is human-maintained and changes rarely. The knowledge base is structured and queryable. The self-learning memory grows automatically with each resolved incident -- the memory writer extracts root cause and remediation, the memory retriever searches it by symptom similarity. Specialists are ephemeral per-incident but draw on all three layers.

diagram:
graph TD
  INC[Incident] --> IC[Incident Commander]
  IC -->|query| BP[Blueprint Layer\nStatic Truth]
  IC -->|query| KB[Knowledge Base\nStructured History]
  IC -->|query| MR[Memory Retriever]
  MR -->|similar incidents| SLM[Self-Learning Memory]
  IC -->|dispatch| S1[Specialist 1]
  IC -->|dispatch| S2[Specialist N]
  S1 -->|findings| IC
  S2 -->|findings| IC
  IC -->|resolution| MW[Memory Writer]
  MW -->|auto-capture| SLM

name: Stateless Message Routing
summary: Task state travels as a portable message through stateless agent workers, eliminating the central orchestrator entirely.
description: Instead of a central orchestrator tracking all workflows, each task's complete state -- conversation history, control flow decisions, execution progress -- is serialized into a portable message object. Agents are stateless workers that pull a message from a distributed queue, apply their role-specific logic, update the state within the message, and route it directly to the next agent. No single process tracks thousands of concurrent workflows. Each task progresses independently as a state machine moving through a pool of interchangeable workers. This inverts the standard orchestrator pattern: the state carries itself through the system rather than being tracked by a coordinator.
realWorldExample: Meta's Matrix framework (2025) implements this for synthetic data generation pipelines. Agents are stateless Ray actors pulling from distributed queues. The 'orchestrator' is a portable state object, not a central process. Result: 2-15x higher throughput than centralized baselines, scaling to 12,400+ concurrent tasks where centralized systems like Coral plateau around 5,000.
whenToUse: When you need to run thousands of concurrent agent workflows and a central orchestrator becomes a bottleneck. When individual tasks are independent and don't need cross-task coordination. When you want horizontal scaling by adding more stateless workers.
strengths: Eliminates the central orchestrator bottleneck entirely
Scales horizontally by adding stateless workers to the pool
Each task is independent -- one failure doesn't cascade to others
Agents are interchangeable and can be replaced or restarted without state loss
weaknesses: Tasks cannot easily coordinate with each other since there is no shared view
The message object must serialize all relevant state, which grows with task complexity
Debugging requires tracing a message's journey through multiple stateless workers
Not suitable for tasks that need real-time cross-workflow decisions or shared context

agents.name: Stateless Worker
agents.description: Pulls a task message from the queue, applies role-specific logic, updates the state, and routes to the next worker

agents.name: Queue Manager
agents.description: Distributes task messages across available workers and handles routing between pipeline stages

shared: Task queue -- distributed message queue holding portable state objects
Completion log -- records of finished task messages for monitoring
private: No persistent private state -- workers are intentionally stateless and interchangeable
memoryArchitecture.description: All state lives in the message itself, not in any agent. The queue is the only shared infrastructure. Workers have zero persistent state by design, enabling horizontal scaling and fault tolerance.

diagram:
graph LR
  T1[Task Message 1] --> Q[(Distributed Queue)]
  T2[Task Message 2] --> Q
  T3[Task Message N] --> Q
  Q --> W1[Worker A]
  Q --> W2[Worker B]
  Q --> W3[Worker C]
  W1 -->|updated message| Q
  W2 -->|updated message| Q
  W3 -->|completed| LOG[Completion Log]

name: Evolutionary Self-Modifier
summary: A population of agents that rewrite their own source code, with evolutionary selection retaining the fittest variants.
description: The system is a single editable Python program containing both a task agent and a meta agent. The meta agent can rewrite any file in the codebase, including itself. A population-based archive maintains multiple program variants. Flat evolutionary search -- not gradient descent, not hierarchy -- explores the space of possible agent programs. The key insight is that given the ability to modify their own code, agents spontaneously develop capabilities that researchers would normally hand-engineer: persistent memory systems, performance tracking classes, evaluation pipelines, and compute-aware exploration strategies. No agent is special; any can be replaced by a fitter variant.
realWorldExample: Meta FAIR Hyperagents (2026) tested this pattern on coding benchmarks (Pass@1 improved from 0.14 to 0.34), paper review (0 to 0.71 accuracy), and robotics reward design. Agents autonomously developed persistent memory files, performance tracking classes, and strategies that allocated more compute to harder problems. The population-based archive maintained diversity without any central coordinator directing the search.
whenToUse: When you want agents to discover their own architectures rather than hand-designing them. Best for research settings where you can afford many evaluation cycles and want to explore the space of possible agent designs. Not suitable for production systems that need predictable behavior.
strengths: Agents discover capabilities (memory, tracking, evaluation) that humans might not think to add
No architectural assumptions -- the search is over the full space of possible programs
Population diversity prevents premature convergence on a single approach
Requires minimal human design -- the evolutionary process does the architecture work
weaknesses: Compute-intensive -- each variant needs full evaluation on the task suite
Unpredictable outputs -- you cannot guarantee what architecture the agents will converge on
No interpretability -- evolved agents may use strategies humans cannot understand
Requires a well-defined fitness function, which is itself a hard design problem

agents.name: Task Agent
agents.description: Executes the target task (coding, review, reward design). Its code is the subject of evolutionary modification

agents.name: Meta Agent
agents.description: Analyzes task agent performance and rewrites source files to improve results. Can modify any file including its own code

agents.name: Population Archive
agents.description: Maintains a diverse set of program variants ranked by fitness. Selects parents for the next generation of modifications

shared: Population archive -- ranked collection of program variants with fitness scores
Fitness log -- evaluation results for each variant on the task suite
private: Source code -- each variant's full program, editable by its meta agent
memoryArchitecture.description: The population archive is the only shared state. Each program variant is an isolated unit of code that the meta agent can freely modify. Coordination happens through selection pressure, not communication -- fitter variants survive, weaker ones are replaced. Agents may spontaneously create their own persistent memory files as an emergent behavior.

diagram:
graph TD
  POP[(Population Archive)] -->|select| V1[Variant 1]
  POP -->|select| V2[Variant 2]
  POP -->|select| V3[Variant N]
  V1 --> M1[Meta Agent 1]
  M1 -->|rewrite code| V1
  V1 --> E1[Evaluate]
  V2 --> M2[Meta Agent 2]
  M2 -->|rewrite code| V2
  V2 --> E2[Evaluate]
  V3 --> M3[Meta Agent N]
  M3 -->|rewrite code| V3
  V3 --> E3[Evaluate]
  E1 -->|fitness| POP
  E2 -->|fitness| POP
  E3 -->|fitness| POP

name: File-Based Agent Memory
summary: Agents persist knowledge across sessions using version-controlled markdown files with an index.
description: Agents maintain persistent state across sessions by reading and writing markdown files organized by type -- user preferences, project context, feedback, references. An index file maps to individual memory files, allowing selective retrieval. At session start, the agent reads its memory; at session end, it writes back what it learned. Because the memory files are plain text in the project repository, they are version-controlled, diffable, and human-readable. This pattern exists because LLM agents are stateless by default -- every session starts fresh. File-based memory solves session amnesia without requiring a database, vector store, or external service.
realWorldExample: Claude Code uses ~/.claude/projects/ directories with MEMORY.md indexes linking to typed memory files (user preferences, feedback, project context, references). Memory persists across sessions and is version-controlled with the project. Cursor uses .cursor/rules files for project-specific agent memory. Windsurf uses .windsurfrules. The forge.thisminute.org project maintains memory/ directories with per-agent memory files updated after each session, enabling coherent multi-week development across dozens of sessions.
whenToUse: When agents need to remember things across sessions -- user preferences, project conventions, past decisions, recurring issues. Essential for any agent that works on a project over days or weeks rather than in a single session. Prefer this over database-backed memory when you want human-readable, version-controlled, and portable memory.
strengths: Human-readable and editable -- users can directly read, modify, or delete agent memories
Version-controlled -- memory changes are tracked alongside code changes in git
No infrastructure -- no database, vector store, or external service required
Portable -- memory travels with the project repository to any machine
weaknesses: Scales poorly -- agents must read entire memory files into context, consuming tokens
No semantic search -- retrieval is by file name and structure, not by relevance
Memory conflicts possible when multiple agents write to the same files concurrently
Memory maintenance is manual -- stale entries accumulate without explicit cleanup

agents.name: Primary Agent
agents.description: The agent that reads memory at session start, performs work, and writes updated memory at session end

agents.name: Memory Index
agents.description: An index file (e.g., MEMORY.md) that maps to individual memory files by type, enabling selective retrieval

shared: Memory index -- maps to typed memory files for selective retrieval
Project memory files -- project-level context shared across agents
private: Per-agent memory files -- individual agent learnings, preferences, and session history
memoryArchitecture.description: Memory is organized as plain markdown files in a directory structure. An index file provides navigation. Files are typed by content (user preferences, feedback, project context, references). Agents read relevant files at session start and write updates at session end. The entire memory directory is version-controlled with the project.

diagram:
graph TD
  S1[Session Start] --> R[Read Memory Index]
  R --> MF1[user-preferences.md]
  R --> MF2[project-context.md]
  R --> MF3[feedback.md]
  MF1 --> CTX[Agent Context]
  MF2 --> CTX
  MF3 --> CTX
  CTX --> WORK[Do Work]
  WORK --> W[Write Updated Memory]
  W --> MF1
  W --> MF2
  W --> IDX[Update Index]
  IDX --> GIT[(Git Repository)]

name: Tool Server Protocol
summary: Agents connect to standardized tool servers that expose capabilities through a shared protocol for dynamic discovery.
description: Instead of hardcoding tools into agent code, tools live on external servers that expose their capabilities through a standardized protocol. Agents discover available tools at runtime by querying servers, then invoke them through a uniform interface. Multiple agents can share the same tool servers, and new tools can be added without modifying any agent. The protocol typically uses JSON-RPC or similar lightweight messaging. This pattern exists because the alternative -- baking every tool into every agent -- creates an N*M integration problem that does not scale. A protocol layer decouples agents from tools, enabling a marketplace of capabilities.
realWorldExample: Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) defines a JSON-RPC 2.0 standard for tool servers that any agent can connect to. Servers expose tools (callable functions), resources (readable data), and prompts (reusable templates). Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, and other IDEs implement MCP clients. The protocol enables a growing ecosystem of community-built servers for databases, APIs, file systems, and cloud services -- all usable by any MCP-compatible agent without custom integration.
whenToUse: When you need agents to use tools from multiple sources, when tool capabilities change frequently, or when multiple agents need to share the same tools. Essential when building an agent ecosystem rather than a single agent. Overkill for a single agent with a fixed set of three tools.
strengths: Decouples agents from tools -- add new tools without touching agent code
Dynamic discovery -- agents learn what tools are available at runtime
Shared infrastructure -- multiple agents reuse the same tool servers
Ecosystem effect -- a standard protocol enables community-built tool servers
weaknesses: Protocol overhead -- every tool call goes through serialization, network, deserialization
Security surface -- tool servers are network-accessible services that need authentication and authorization
Discovery complexity -- agents must decide which tools to use from a potentially large catalog
Versioning challenges -- tool servers can change their interfaces, breaking agents that depend on them

agents.name: Agent Client
agents.description: An LLM agent that discovers and invokes tools through the protocol. Maintains connections to one or more tool servers

agents.name: Tool Server
agents.description: Exposes a set of tools, resources, and prompts through the standardized protocol. Can be a local process, remote service, or container

agents.name: Server Registry
agents.description: Optional central registry where tool servers advertise their capabilities for agent discovery

shared: Server registry -- catalog of available tool servers and their capabilities
Protocol schema -- shared definition of how tools are described, invoked, and respond
private: Agent tool preferences -- which tools the agent has used successfully before
Server connection state -- active sessions with tool servers
memoryArchitecture.description: Tool servers are the shared infrastructure. Each server maintains its own state (database connections, API keys, file handles). Agents connect to servers through the protocol and invoke tools through a uniform interface. The registry provides discovery. There is no shared state between agents beyond the servers they both connect to.

diagram:
graph TD
  A1[Agent 1] -->|JSON-RPC| TS1[Tool Server:\nDatabase]
  A1 -->|JSON-RPC| TS2[Tool Server:\nFile System]
  A2[Agent 2] -->|JSON-RPC| TS2
  A2 -->|JSON-RPC| TS3[Tool Server:\nCloud API]
  A3[Agent 3] -->|JSON-RPC| TS1
  A3 -->|JSON-RPC| TS3
  REG[(Server Registry)] -.->|discover| A1
  REG -.->|discover| A2
  REG -.->|discover| A3
  TS1 -.->|advertise| REG
  TS2 -.->|advertise| REG
  TS3 -.->|advertise| REG

name: Progressive Context Disclosure
summary: Agents start with minimal context and progressively load more as needed, keeping large data outside the prompt.
description: Instead of front-loading all relevant information into the prompt, the agent begins with a minimal context and queries for additional information only when needed. Large datasets, logs, and intermediate results are stored in external files or sessions rather than entering the context window. The agent writes analysis code (e.g., pandas queries) that runs against external data and returns only summarized results. This pattern exists because context windows are expensive and finite -- dumping 50K tokens of logs into a prompt wastes tokens and degrades model attention. Progressive disclosure trades latency (extra tool calls) for dramatically better token efficiency and output quality.
realWorldExample: Microsoft's Azure SRE Agent (2025) sends monitoring data to a code interpreter where the model writes pandas analysis, instead of dumping 50K tokens of telemetry into the prompt. File sessions hold large outputs outside context. This reduced context overhead by 60-70% while improving diagnostic accuracy. Google ADK's context engineering separates durable state (persists across calls) from per-call working context (loaded on demand). The pattern is also central to RAG architectures, which retrieve relevant chunks rather than loading entire documents.
whenToUse: When agents work with large datasets, logs, codebases, or documents that exceed what fits comfortably in a context window. When you observe degraded output quality from long contexts. When token costs are a concern. Not needed for simple tasks where all relevant information fits in a few thousand tokens.
strengths: Dramatically reduces token usage -- only relevant information enters the context
Improves output quality -- models attend better to shorter, more focused contexts
Scales to arbitrarily large data -- external storage has no token limit
Enables iterative analysis -- the agent can query data multiple times from different angles
weaknesses: Higher latency -- each progressive load requires a tool call round-trip
Agent must know what to ask for -- poor query strategies miss relevant information
Complexity in managing external state -- file sessions, code interpreters, and caches add infrastructure
Risk of under-loading -- the agent may miss critical context it did not know to request

agents.name: Reasoning Agent
agents.description: The primary LLM agent that maintains a minimal context and decides what additional information to load through tool calls

agents.name: Data Session
agents.description: An external execution environment (code interpreter, file session) that holds large data and returns summarized results to the agent

agents.name: Context Retriever
agents.description: Fetches specific slices of data on demand -- file sections, log ranges, database query results, search results

shared: External data store -- large datasets, logs, and documents stored outside the context window
Summary cache -- previously computed summaries available for re-use
private: Working context -- the agent's current, minimal prompt with only the information it has loaded so far
memoryArchitecture.description: The core principle is that most data lives outside the context window. The agent's context contains only a working set of summaries and results. Large data resides in external sessions (code interpreters, file systems) where the agent can query it through tool calls. Each query returns only the relevant slice, keeping the context focused.

diagram:
graph TD
  Q[User Query] --> RA[Reasoning Agent\nMinimal Context]
  RA -->|need more data| R[Context Retriever]
  R -->|relevant slice| RA
  RA -->|write analysis code| DS[Data Session\nCode Interpreter]
  DS -->|summary result| RA
  RA -->|need specific file| FS[(External Files)]
  FS -->|file section| RA
  RA --> OUT[Response]
  DB[(Large Dataset\n50K+ tokens)] -.-> DS
  LOGS[(System Logs)] -.-> R

name: Hook-Driven Agent Lifecycle
summary: Shell commands execute automatically at agent lifecycle events, enabling extensibility without modifying the agent.
description: The agent system exposes lifecycle events -- before/after tool calls, session start/stop, notifications, errors -- and users configure shell commands that execute in response. Hooks are declared in configuration, not in agent code, making them a clean extension point. This pattern exists because agents need to interact with surrounding development workflows (linters, formatters, CI systems, notification services) but baking those interactions into the agent creates coupling and rigidity. Hooks decouple the agent from its environment: the agent does not know or care what hooks are configured, and hooks do not modify the agent's core behavior.
realWorldExample: Claude Code's hooks system executes user-configured shell commands at PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Notification, and Stop events. Users configure hooks in .claude/settings.json to auto-lint after file edits, block dangerous commands before execution, log all tool use to a file, or send notifications on task completion. The hooks run as subprocesses and can modify, block, or observe agent actions without changing the agent itself. Git hooks (pre-commit, post-merge) are the analogous pattern in version control systems.
whenToUse: When you need to integrate agent behavior with external workflows (linting, testing, notifications, logging) without modifying the agent itself. When different environments need different behaviors from the same agent. When you want users to customize agent behavior declaratively rather than through code changes.
strengths: Clean separation -- agent behavior and environment integration are fully decoupled
Declarative configuration -- hooks are defined in settings files, not code
Composable -- multiple hooks can respond to the same event independently
User-controlled -- the end user configures hooks to match their workflow, not the agent developer
weaknesses: Hook failures can block agent execution if not handled gracefully
Debugging is harder when behavior depends on external shell commands
Security risk -- hooks execute arbitrary shell commands with the agent's permissions
Ordering complexity -- when multiple hooks respond to the same event, execution order matters

agents.name: Primary Agent
agents.description: The agent that emits lifecycle events. Unaware of what hooks are configured -- it just does its work and the hook system intercepts at defined points

agents.name: Hook Executor
agents.description: Monitors lifecycle events and executes configured shell commands. Can pass event data to hooks and receive pass/fail signals back

shared: Hook configuration -- declarative rules mapping lifecycle events to shell commands
Event log -- record of lifecycle events and hook execution results
private: Agent context -- the primary agent's working state, unaffected by hooks
memoryArchitecture.description: The hook executor reads configuration at startup and subscribes to lifecycle events. When an event fires, it executes the matching shell commands as subprocesses. Hook results (pass/fail, stdout) can influence agent behavior (blocking a tool call) or simply observe (logging). The agent itself has no knowledge of hooks.

diagram:
graph TD
  CFG[Hook Config\n.claude/settings.json] --> HE[Hook Executor]
  AG[Primary Agent] -->|PreToolUse| HE
  HE -->|run lint| SH1[Shell: eslint]
  HE -->|block dangerous cmd| SH2[Shell: validator]
  AG -->|PostToolUse| HE
  HE -->|auto-format| SH3[Shell: prettier]
  AG -->|Notification| HE
  HE -->|send alert| SH4[Shell: notify-send]
  AG -->|Stop| HE
  HE -->|cleanup| SH5[Shell: cleanup.sh]
  SH1 -->|pass/fail| AG
  SH2 -->|block/allow| AG

name: Worktree Isolation
summary: Each agent gets an isolated git worktree, enabling safe parallel work on the same codebase without conflicts.
description: When an agent needs to make changes that might conflict with the main workspace or with other agents, it receives a git worktree -- a full, independent copy of the repository with its own working directory and branch. The agent works freely in isolation: editing files, running tests, making commits. When finished, changes are returned to the parent for review and merge. Multiple agents can work on different worktrees simultaneously without stepping on each other's files. This pattern exists because shared-filesystem concurrency is the hardest problem in multi-agent coding systems. Worktrees solve it by eliminating the shared filesystem entirely -- each agent gets its own.
realWorldExample: Claude Code's subagent spawning gives each spawned agent its own git worktree -- a full independent copy of the repo on a separate branch. The subagent works freely without affecting the parent's workspace. On completion, it returns the worktree path and branch name for the parent to inspect and merge. This enables patterns like 'implement feature A and feature B in parallel' where both agents edit the same files without conflicts. Git worktrees are a native git feature (git worktree add) that creates lightweight linked copies of a repository.
whenToUse: When multiple agents need to edit the same codebase simultaneously. When you want to isolate risky or experimental changes from the main workspace. When an agent's work might need to be discarded without affecting other work in progress. Not needed for read-only tasks or sequential single-agent workflows.
strengths: Complete isolation -- agents cannot corrupt each other's working directories
Native git integration -- worktrees are a built-in git feature with full branch/commit support
Lightweight -- worktrees share the git object store, not full repository copies
Safe experimentation -- failed attempts are discarded by deleting the worktree
weaknesses: Merge conflicts still occur when worktrees are combined -- isolation delays but does not eliminate conflicts
Disk space scales linearly with the number of active worktrees
Agents lose real-time awareness of what other agents are doing in their worktrees
Orchestration complexity -- the parent must manage worktree lifecycle (create, monitor, merge, cleanup)

agents.name: Parent Agent
agents.description: Creates worktrees, dispatches tasks to subagents, reviews results, and merges changes back into the main workspace

agents.name: Isolated Subagent
agents.description: Works in its own git worktree on a dedicated branch. Has full read-write access to its copy of the codebase. Returns results via the worktree path and branch

shared: Git object store -- the shared repository that all worktrees are linked to
Merge queue -- completed worktrees awaiting parent review and integration
private: Worktree -- each subagent's isolated copy of the repository with its own working directory and branch
memoryArchitecture.description: Each subagent has a private filesystem (the worktree) but shares the underlying git object store. Changes are isolated until explicitly merged. The parent agent manages the lifecycle: creating worktrees before dispatch, monitoring progress, and merging or discarding results after completion. No inter-subagent communication exists -- all coordination goes through the parent.

diagram:
graph TD
  PA[Parent Agent\nMain Workspace] -->|create worktree| WT1[Worktree 1\nBranch: feature-a]
  PA -->|create worktree| WT2[Worktree 2\nBranch: feature-b]
  PA -->|create worktree| WT3[Worktree 3\nBranch: fix-c]
  SA1[Subagent 1] --> WT1
  SA2[Subagent 2] --> WT2
  SA3[Subagent 3] --> WT3
  WT1 -->|done: return branch| PA
  WT2 -->|done: return branch| PA
  WT3 -->|done: return branch| PA
  PA -->|merge| MAIN[(Main Branch)]
  GIT[(Shared Git Objects)] -.-> WT1
  GIT -.-> WT2
  GIT -.-> WT3

name: LLM-as-Judge
summary: A dedicated LLM evaluates other agents' outputs against explicit criteria, acting as an automated quality gate.
description: A separate LLM instance -- deliberately different from the producing agent in model, prompt, or temperature -- evaluates outputs against explicit criteria and produces structured scores with explanations. The judge is not the same agent grading its own work; the architectural separation prevents self-serving bias. The pattern serves two purposes: real-time quality gates (blocking bad outputs before they reach users) and offline evaluation (measuring system quality over time). The key insight is that LLMs are better at evaluating quality than generating it perfectly on the first try, so separating generation from evaluation and iterating between them produces better results than either alone.
realWorldExample: MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena use GPT-4 as a judge to evaluate model responses on a 1-10 scale, with high correlation to human preferences. Anthropic's constitutional AI uses evaluation criteria derived from principles. Amazon's agent evaluation framework positions LLM-as-judge as a core architectural component alongside ground-truth benchmarks. The pattern is standard in RLHF pipelines where a reward model (effectively a judge) scores generations. McKinsey/QuantumBlack's ARK framework uses a Critic Agent with veto authority that blocks outputs failing a definition-of-done checklist.
whenToUse: When you need automated quality evaluation that is cheaper than human review but more nuanced than rule-based checks. When building evaluation pipelines for agent systems. When you want a quality gate between agent output and user delivery. Not appropriate when ground-truth labels exist and simple accuracy metrics suffice.
strengths: More nuanced evaluation than rule-based checks -- can assess coherence, relevance, and tone
Scalable -- can evaluate thousands of outputs without human reviewers
Structured output -- scores and explanations enable both gating and debugging
Cheap iteration -- generation + judge evaluation is faster and cheaper than human review loops
weaknesses: Judge bias -- LLM judges favor verbose, confident-sounding outputs regardless of accuracy
Self-consistency issues -- the same judge can give different scores to the same output on different runs
Correlation with human judgment is imperfect -- high agreement on average but unreliable on edge cases
Gaming risk -- if generators know the judge criteria, they can optimize for scores rather than quality

agents.name: Generator Agent
agents.description: Produces the output to be evaluated. May be a single agent or a multi-agent system whose final output is judged

agents.name: Judge Agent
agents.description: A separate LLM (different model, prompt, or temperature) that evaluates generator output against explicit criteria and produces structured scores with explanations

agents.name: Criteria Manager
agents.description: Maintains and updates the evaluation criteria, rubrics, and scoring guidelines used by the judge

shared: Evaluation criteria -- rubrics and scoring guidelines defining what 'good' means
Score registry -- historical scores and explanations for trend analysis
private: Judge calibration -- the judge's system prompt and few-shot examples, kept separate from generators
Generator context -- the generator's working state, isolated from judge criteria to prevent gaming
memoryArchitecture.description: Generators and judge are architecturally separated -- they do not share context, system prompts, or state. The evaluation criteria are the shared contract. Generators submit outputs to the evaluation queue. The judge scores them independently. Scores feed back into the system for quality gating or training. The separation prevents the judge from being influenced by the generator's reasoning.

diagram:
graph TD
  IN[Input Task] --> G1[Generator 1]
  IN --> G2[Generator 2]
  IN --> G3[Generator 3]
  G1 -->|output| EQ[(Evaluation Queue)]
  G2 -->|output| EQ
  G3 -->|output| EQ
  EQ --> J[Judge Agent\nDifferent Model]
  CR[Criteria & Rubrics] --> J
  J -->|score + explanation| SR[(Score Registry)]
  J -->|pass/fail| GATE{Quality Gate}
  GATE -->|pass| OUT[Deliver to User]
  GATE -->|fail| RETRY[Retry/Revise]

name: Agent Handoff Chain
summary: Agents pass control to each other through explicit handoffs carrying full conversation context.
description: Agents transfer responsibility to each other through a first-class handoff primitive that carries the full conversation context. Each agent specializes in a different domain or phase. When an agent determines it is not the right one for the current task, it hands off to a more appropriate agent -- not by routing through a central orchestrator, but by directly transferring control and context. The receiving agent sees the entire conversation history and picks up seamlessly. This pattern exists as a deliberate simplification: rather than building complex graph orchestrators or routing layers, you compose agent systems from simple, chainable handoffs. The handoff is the only coordination primitive needed.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's Agents SDK makes handoffs a core primitive -- agents declare transfer functions that pass full conversation context to the receiving agent. This evolved from their experimental Swarm framework into a production SDK. The receiving agent sees the entire history and takes over the conversation. Anthropic's tool_use pattern enables similar handoffs where one agent calls another as a tool, passing context through the tool input.
