The pattern deliberately avoids complex graph orchestration in favor of simple, composable transfers.
whenToUse: When you have multiple specialized agents and need clean transitions between them -- customer service escalation, multi-phase workflows, domain routing. When you want the simplicity of a pipeline but the flexibility of any-to-any connections. Prefer this over graph orchestration when the coordination logic is simple enough to express as agent-to-agent transfers.
strengths: Minimal coordination overhead -- handoff is the only primitive, no graph or router needed
Full context preservation -- the receiving agent sees the entire conversation history
Easy to reason about -- each handoff is a clear transfer of responsibility
Composable -- agents can be added or removed without restructuring the system
weaknesses: Context accumulation -- each handoff carries the full history, which grows with each transfer
No global view -- no single agent sees the overall workflow or can optimize across handoffs
Handoff loops -- agents can hand off to each other indefinitely without making progress
Debugging requires tracing through multiple agents' decision points to understand why a handoff occurred

agents.name: Triage Agent
agents.description: Initial point of contact that assesses the task and hands off to the appropriate specialist agent

agents.name: Specialist Agent
agents.description: Domain-specialized agent that handles tasks in its area. Can hand off to other specialists or back to triage if the task shifts domains

shared: Conversation history -- the full thread carried through each handoff
Handoff log -- record of which agent handed off to which, with reasons
private: Agent specialization -- each agent's domain-specific system prompt and tool set
memoryArchitecture.description: The conversation history is the shared state, passed in full with each handoff. There is no external state store or database -- the context travels with the handoff. Each agent's specialization (system prompt, available tools) is private. The handoff log provides observability into the transfer chain.

diagram:
graph LR
  U[User] --> T[Triage Agent]
  T -->|handoff + context| S1[Specialist:\nBilling]
  T -->|handoff + context| S2[Specialist:\nTechnical]
  S1 -->|handoff + context| S3[Specialist:\nRefunds]
  S2 -->|handoff + context| S1
  S3 -->|response| U
  S2 -->|response| U
  CTX[(Full Conversation\nContext)] -.->|carried with\neach handoff| T
  CTX -.-> S1
  CTX -.-> S2
  CTX -.-> S3

name: Docker-Sandboxed Execution
summary: Agent code execution happens inside disposable containers with limited access, enabling safe execution of untrusted code.
description: Code generated or invoked by agents runs inside isolated, disposable containers rather than on the host system. Each execution gets a fresh environment with restricted filesystem access, network limitations, and resource caps (CPU, memory, time). Results are extracted through a defined interface, and the container is destroyed after execution. This pattern exists because agents that write and run code are inherently dangerous -- a single hallucinated rm -rf or an infinite loop could destroy data or consume all resources. Sandboxing makes code execution safe by default, turning 'can the agent run code?' from a security nightmare into a standard capability.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's Code Interpreter runs Python in a sandboxed container with pre-installed libraries, a virtual filesystem, and session persistence within a conversation. E2B (open-source) provides cloud-based sandboxed environments specifically designed for AI agent code execution, with SDKs for Python and JavaScript. Meta's Hyperagents run each evaluation in a Docker container to isolate potentially dangerous evolved code. Claude Code uses subprocess sandboxing with configurable permissions (network access, filesystem scope) to constrain tool execution.
whenToUse: Whenever agents generate and execute code, especially from untrusted or user-provided inputs. Essential for code interpreter features, agent evaluation pipelines, and any system where agent-generated code touches production resources. The overhead is justified any time code execution could have side effects.
strengths: Security by default -- untrusted code cannot damage the host or access sensitive data
Reproducible environments -- each execution starts from a known-good state
Resource limits prevent runaway code from consuming all CPU, memory, or disk
Disposable -- failed or malicious executions are cleaned up automatically
weaknesses: Startup latency -- container creation adds 100ms-2s per execution depending on image size
Limited access -- sandboxed code cannot interact with host services, databases, or APIs without explicit tunneling
State loss -- each execution starts fresh, requiring explicit serialization to persist results across runs
Infrastructure cost -- running containers at scale requires container orchestration (Docker, Kubernetes)

agents.name: Execution Orchestrator
agents.description: Receives code from the LLM agent, creates a sandbox container, executes the code, extracts results, and destroys the container

agents.name: Sandbox Container
agents.description: A disposable container with a controlled environment -- pre-installed libraries, restricted filesystem, network limits, resource caps. Executes code and returns results through a defined interface

agents.name: Code-Writing Agent
agents.description: The LLM agent that generates code to be executed. Receives results from the sandbox and incorporates them into its reasoning

shared: Execution log -- record of code executions, results, and resource usage
Container image -- the base environment with pre-installed libraries and tools
private: Sandbox filesystem -- the container's isolated filesystem, destroyed after execution
Agent context -- the LLM agent's working state, including code generation history
memoryArchitecture.description: The sandbox is intentionally stateless -- each execution starts from the container image. Results must be explicitly extracted through the defined interface (stdout, files, exit code). The orchestrator maintains execution logs for debugging. The agent sees only the extracted results, not the sandbox internals. Session persistence (keeping a container alive across multiple executions) is an optional extension that trades isolation for convenience.

diagram:
graph TD
  AG[Code-Writing Agent] -->|generated code| ORCH[Execution Orchestrator]
  ORCH -->|create| SB[Sandbox Container\nRestricted FS\nNo Network\nResource Caps]
  SB -->|execute code| RUN[Run in Isolation]
  RUN -->|stdout, files| ORCH
  RUN -->|exit code| ORCH
  ORCH -->|results| AG
  ORCH -->|destroy| SB
  IMG[(Container Image\nPre-installed Libs)] -.-> SB
  LOG[(Execution Log)] -.-> ORCH

name: Checkpoint-Resume Across Sessions
summary: Agents write structured checkpoints at session end and resume from them in the next session, enabling multi-session continuity.
description: When a session ends -- whether from context limits, user departure, timeout, or crash -- the agent writes a structured checkpoint capturing what was completed, what is in progress, what is deferred, and any uncommitted state. The next session reads the checkpoint and picks up where the previous session left off. The checkpoint is effectively a handoff from the agent's past self to its future self. This pattern exists because LLM agent sessions are inherently finite (context windows, rate limits, user availability) but real work spans days or weeks. Without checkpointing, every session starts from scratch, rediscovering context that was already known. Checkpoints turn finite sessions into a continuous workflow.
realWorldExample: The forge.thisminute.org project uses .claude/checkpoint.md to persist multi-session state across 9+ sessions. Each session reads the checkpoint, does work, and updates it at shutdown, enabling coherent multi-week development despite each session starting fresh. Claude Code's auto-compact system summarizes context when approaching the token limit, creating implicit checkpoints that preserve continuity within a session. Temporal and Restate provide durable execution frameworks where agent state is checkpointed to external storage, surviving crashes and rate limits.
whenToUse: When work spans multiple sessions and context must persist between them. When sessions might end unexpectedly (crashes, rate limits, context exhaustion). When you want agents to build on prior work rather than rediscovering it each time. Not needed for single-session tasks that complete within one interaction.
strengths: Enables multi-session continuity -- agents build on prior work instead of starting fresh
Crash recovery -- even abrupt termination is recoverable if the last checkpoint is recent
Human-readable -- structured checkpoints let users see exactly where the agent left off
Low overhead -- writing a checkpoint at session end adds minimal time to each session
weaknesses: Checkpoint staleness -- if the world changes between sessions, the checkpoint may contain outdated assumptions
Lossy compression -- checkpoints summarize rather than preserve full context, so details are lost
No automatic conflict detection -- if a human makes changes between sessions, the checkpoint may be inconsistent
Maintenance burden -- checkpoints accumulate if not regularly cleaned up or archived

agents.name: Session Agent
agents.description: The agent that reads checkpoints at session start, performs work, and writes updated checkpoints at session end or when approaching context limits

agents.name: Checkpoint Writer
agents.description: A specialized routine that captures session state into a structured format: completed items, in-progress work, deferred tasks, uncommitted changes, and key decisions

shared: Checkpoint file -- structured document capturing session state for the next session to read
Session log -- optional record of actions taken during each session for debugging
private: Working context -- the agent's current session state, which gets compressed into the checkpoint at session end
memoryArchitecture.description: The checkpoint file is the bridge between sessions. It is written at session end and read at session start. The format is structured (what is done, what is in progress, what is deferred) rather than a raw context dump, so it survives context loss. The agent treats the checkpoint as a self-handoff: write instructions for your future self, then your future self reads them and continues.

diagram:
graph TD
  S1[Session N] -->|read checkpoint| CP[(Checkpoint File\nDone / In Progress / Deferred)]
  S1 -->|do work| WORK[Work]
  WORK -->|session ending| CW[Checkpoint Writer]
  CW -->|write updated checkpoint| CP
  CP -->|read checkpoint| S2[Session N+1]
  S2 -->|continue work| WORK2[Work]
  WORK2 -->|session ending| CW2[Checkpoint Writer]
  CW2 -->|write updated checkpoint| CP
  CP -->|read checkpoint| S3[Session N+2]
  GIT[(Git Repository)] -.->|version controlled| CP

name: Red-Team Blue-Team Loop
summary: A red-team agent probes for failures while a blue-team agent defends and patches, iteratively hardening the system.
description: A red-team agent actively probes for vulnerabilities -- jailbreaks, hallucinations, edge cases, safety violations, prompt injections -- while a blue-team agent defends, patches, and hardens the system against discovered attacks. The loop iterates: the red team escalates with novel attack vectors, the blue team strengthens defenses, and neither side converges because both are improving. This is fundamentally different from debate-protocol (which seeks truth through argumentation) -- this pattern is about adversarial hardening through attack and defense. The red team's objective is to break things; the blue team's objective is to make things unbreakable. An optional judge or scoring system tracks whether the overall system is getting more robust over time.
realWorldExample: Anthropic's red-teaming methodology uses dedicated teams to probe Claude for safety violations, with findings feeding directly into training and guardrail improvements. OpenAI's Preparedness Framework formalizes red-teaming into risk categories (cybersecurity, CBRN, persuasion, model autonomy) with scored evaluations. Microsoft's PyRIT (Python Risk Identification Toolkit for generative AI) automates red-teaming with attack strategies and scoring. Haize Labs automates red-teaming at scale, generating adversarial prompts and evaluating model responses programmatically.
whenToUse: When you need to systematically discover and fix vulnerabilities in an LLM system before deployment. When safety and robustness are non-negotiable requirements. When manual testing cannot cover the combinatorial space of possible attacks. Not needed for low-stakes applications where occasional failures are acceptable.
strengths: Discovers vulnerabilities that manual testing and static analysis miss
Iterative escalation means defenses improve continuously, not just once
Automated red-teaming scales to cover attack surfaces that humans cannot manually test
Creates a measurable hardening trajectory -- you can track defense improvement over time
weaknesses: Red-team agents may find attacks that are unrealistic in production but waste blue-team effort
Arms race dynamics -- both sides escalate complexity, increasing system maintenance cost
Red-team agents need to be as capable as real attackers, which requires significant investment
False sense of security -- passing red-team tests does not guarantee safety against novel attacks

agents.name: Red-Team Agent
agents.description: Generates adversarial inputs, jailbreak attempts, edge cases, and attack vectors designed to break or bypass the target system's defenses

agents.name: Blue-Team Agent
agents.description: Analyzes red-team attacks, develops patches and guardrails, and strengthens the target system against discovered vulnerabilities

agents.name: Scorekeeper
agents.description: Tracks attack success rates, defense coverage, and overall system robustness over iterations. Determines when hardening goals are met

shared: Robustness dashboard -- aggregated metrics on attack success rates, defense coverage, and hardening trajectory
Vulnerability registry -- catalog of discovered attack vectors with severity and patch status
private: Red-team playbook -- attack strategies and techniques, kept private so blue team cannot pre-optimize for known attacks
Blue-team defenses -- guardrail configurations and patch logic, kept private so red team cannot reverse-engineer them
memoryArchitecture.description: Red and blue teams are deliberately isolated -- neither sees the other's private strategies. The shared vulnerability registry and robustness dashboard are the only points of convergence. The scorekeeper mediates, tracking whether attacks succeed and whether patches hold. This isolation is critical: if the red team sees blue-team defenses, it cannot simulate a realistic attacker.

diagram:
graph TD
  RT1[Red-Team Agent 1] -->|attack vector| TGT[Target System]
  RT2[Red-Team Agent 2] -->|jailbreak attempt| TGT
  RT3[Red-Team Agent 3] -->|edge case| TGT
  TGT -->|vulnerability found| AR[(Attack Reports)]
  AR --> BT[Blue-Team Agent]
  BT -->|patch/guardrail| TGT
  BT -->|defense update| DP[(Defense Patches)]
  AR --> SK[Scorekeeper]
  DP --> SK
  SK -->|robustness score| DASH[(Robustness Dashboard)]
  DASH -.->|next round targets| RT1
  DASH -.->|next round targets| RT2

name: Guardrail Sandwich
summary: Input guardrails validate before the agent acts, output guardrails validate before the user sees the result.
description: User input passes through an input guardrail layer (topical filtering, injection detection, content classification) before reaching the agent. The agent operates freely with its full capabilities. Agent output then passes through an output guardrail layer (safety checks, PII detection, hallucination flagging, format validation) before reaching the user. The agent is sandwiched between two validation layers that it does not control. Guardrails can be rule-based (regex, blocklists), classifier-based (fine-tuned models), or LLM-based (a separate model evaluating content). The key architectural insight is that separating validation from generation lets each layer be independently updated, tested, and configured without modifying the agent itself.
realWorldExample: NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails uses Colang, a modeling language, to define topical rails (keep the agent on-topic), safety rails (block harmful content), and security rails (prevent prompt injection). Guardrails AI provides a similar framework with validators for hallucination, toxicity, PII, and custom business rules. OpenAI Agents SDK has input and output guardrails as first-class primitives that run in parallel with agent execution. Anthropic's usage policy enforcement applies content filtering as a layer separate from Claude's core capabilities.
whenToUse: When deploying LLM agents to production where safety, compliance, or content quality requirements exist. When you need to enforce policies without modifying the agent's core behavior. When different deployments need different guardrail configurations (e.g., stricter for consumer vs. enterprise). Essential for any user-facing agent system.
strengths: Clean separation of concerns -- agent logic and safety logic are independently maintained
Layered defense -- input and output guardrails catch different classes of problems
Configurable per-deployment -- different environments can have different guardrail strictness
Independently testable -- guardrails can be unit-tested with known-bad inputs without invoking the full agent
weaknesses: Latency overhead -- each guardrail layer adds processing time, especially LLM-based guardrails
False positives -- overly strict guardrails block legitimate requests, degrading user experience
Bypass risk -- sophisticated attacks can craft inputs that pass input guardrails but produce harmful outputs
Maintenance burden -- guardrail rules need continuous updating as new attack patterns emerge

agents.name: Input Guardrail
agents.description: Validates, classifies, and optionally transforms user input before it reaches the agent. Blocks off-topic requests, detects injection attempts, and enforces input policies

agents.name: Core Agent
agents.description: The primary LLM agent that processes validated input and generates responses. Operates freely between the guardrail layers without knowledge of their configuration

agents.name: Output Guardrail
agents.description: Validates agent output before delivery to the user. Checks for safety violations, PII leakage, hallucination markers, and format compliance. Can block, redact, or request regeneration

shared: Guardrail configuration -- rules, classifiers, and thresholds for input and output validation
Violations log -- record of blocked inputs and flagged outputs for monitoring and rule refinement
private: Agent context -- the core agent's conversation state, untouched by guardrails
Classifier models -- fine-tuned models used by guardrail layers for content classification
memoryArchitecture.description: The guardrail layers are stateless validators -- they process each message independently without conversation history. The core agent maintains its own context. The violations log is the only shared observability point, used to monitor guardrail effectiveness and tune rules. Guardrail configuration is managed separately from agent configuration.

diagram:
graph TD
  U[User Input] --> IG[Input Guardrail\nTopical Filter\nInjection Detection\nContent Classifier]
  IG -->|blocked| BLOCK[Blocked Response]
  IG -->|validated input| CA[Core Agent\nFull LLM Capabilities]
  CA -->|raw output| OG[Output Guardrail\nSafety Check\nPII Detection\nFormat Validation]
  OG -->|blocked| REGEN[Request Regeneration]
  REGEN --> CA
  OG -->|validated output| RESP[Response to User]
  CFG[(Guardrail Config\nRules & Thresholds)] -.-> IG
  CFG -.-> OG
  LOG[(Violations Log)] -.-> IG
  LOG -.-> OG

name: Agent Observability Pipeline
summary: Every agent action is traced with structured telemetry enabling replay, debugging, cost tracking, and optimization.
description: Every agent action -- tool calls, LLM invocations, decisions, handoffs, errors -- is captured as structured telemetry with hierarchical trace IDs (session -> turn -> tool call -> LLM request). Each trace records inputs, outputs, latency, token usage, cost, and success/failure. Traces are shipped to a backend that supports querying, visualization, and alerting. This pattern exists because multi-agent systems are opaque by default: when something goes wrong, you cannot tell which agent failed, which tool call returned bad data, or which LLM invocation hallucinated. Observability makes the internal behavior of agent systems as visible as the internal behavior of microservices. Without it, debugging agent failures requires reproducing the entire conversation from scratch.
realWorldExample: LangSmith provides tracing, evaluation, and monitoring for LangChain agents -- every chain step is a span with inputs, outputs, and metadata. Braintrust logs LLM calls with scores for evaluation and comparison. Arize Phoenix provides open-source LLM observability with trace visualization and drift detection. OpenTelemetry is being extended with LLM-specific semantic conventions for spans (model name, token counts, prompt template). Helicone proxies LLM API calls transparently, adding logging and cost tracking without code changes.
whenToUse: When operating agent systems in production where you need to debug failures, track costs, measure latency, and optimize performance. When you need to demonstrate compliance or audit agent behavior. When building evaluation pipelines that compare agent performance across versions. Not needed for simple prototypes or single-prompt applications.
strengths: Makes opaque agent behavior visible -- you can trace exactly what happened and why
Cost attribution -- track token usage and API costs per agent, per tool, per conversation
Enables replay and debugging -- reproduce failures by replaying traces with the same inputs
Supports optimization -- identify expensive or slow steps and target them for improvement
weaknesses: Instrumentation overhead -- adding tracing to every agent action requires code changes or proxy layers
Data volume -- production agent systems generate massive amounts of trace data that requires storage and management
Privacy concerns -- traces capture full inputs and outputs, which may contain sensitive user data
Alert fatigue -- without careful threshold tuning, observability systems generate noise rather than signal

agents.name: Instrumented Agent
agents.description: Any agent whose actions are wrapped with tracing instrumentation. Each tool call, LLM invocation, and decision point emits a structured span

agents.name: Trace Collector
agents.description: Receives spans from instrumented agents, assembles them into hierarchical traces, and ships them to the observability backend

agents.name: Observability Dashboard
agents.description: Provides querying, visualization, and alerting over collected traces. Surfaces cost, latency, error rates, and quality metrics

shared: Trace store -- hierarchical structured logs of all agent actions with timing, costs, and metadata
Alert rules -- thresholds and conditions that trigger notifications when agent behavior degrades
private: Agent context -- the instrumented agent's working state, unaffected by observation
Collector buffer -- in-flight spans being assembled into complete traces before storage
memoryArchitecture.description: The observability pipeline is a passive observer -- it reads agent behavior without modifying it. Spans flow from agents to the collector to the store. The dashboard queries the store. No data flows back from the observability system to the agents unless explicitly configured (e.g., cost-based throttling). The separation between observation and execution is critical for reliability.

diagram:
graph TD
  A1[Agent 1] -->|span| TC[Trace Collector]
  A2[Agent 2] -->|span| TC
  A3[Agent 3] -->|span| TC
  TC -->|assemble traces| TS[(Trace Store)]
  TS --> DASH[Observability Dashboard\nCost / Latency / Errors]
  DASH -->|alert| OPS[Ops Team]
  subgraph Trace Structure
    S[Session Span] --> T1[Turn 1 Span]
    T1 --> TC1[Tool Call Span]
    TC1 --> LLM1[LLM Request Span\nTokens: 1.2K\nLatency: 800ms\nCost: $0.003]
    T1 --> TC2[Tool Call Span]
  end

name: Few-Shot Exemplar Bank
summary: The agent retrieves relevant few-shot examples from a curated bank rather than using hardcoded prompt examples.
description: Instead of embedding fixed few-shot examples in the prompt, the system maintains a curated bank of examples indexed by task type, difficulty, domain, or input similarity. When a new task arrives, the most relevant examples are retrieved from the bank and injected into the prompt dynamically. This decouples prompt engineering from agent logic -- the prompt template stays fixed, but the examples change per-request. The bank can be populated manually (curated gold examples), automatically (successful past interactions), or through optimization (selecting examples that maximize downstream performance). The key insight is that few-shot example selection has an outsized effect on LLM performance, and static example sets underperform dynamic, task-matched retrieval.
realWorldExample: DSPy's example optimization retrieves and optimizes few-shot examples programmatically -- its bootstrapping algorithms generate examples from a training set and select the ones that maximize a metric. Amazon Bedrock's prompt management stores and versions prompt templates with variable slots for dynamic example injection. Many production systems maintain curated example databases indexed by embedding similarity, retrieving the closest examples for each new input. KATE (kNN-Augmented in-context Task-adaptive Example selection) demonstrated that dynamically selected examples significantly outperform random selection.
whenToUse: When few-shot prompting is central to your agent's performance but different inputs need different examples. When you have a large library of potential examples and need to select the most relevant ones. When you want to improve prompts without modifying agent code. Not needed when zero-shot performance is sufficient or when the task space is narrow enough for a fixed example set.
strengths: Dynamic example selection outperforms static examples across diverse task distributions
Decouples prompt engineering from agent code -- examples are data, not hardcoded strings
Enables continuous improvement -- add new examples to the bank without changing the agent
Scalable -- the bank can hold thousands of examples covering edge cases and rare domains
weaknesses: Retrieval quality depends on the indexing strategy -- poor embeddings or metadata produce poor matches
Bank curation is ongoing work -- stale, wrong, or low-quality examples degrade performance
Adds latency -- example retrieval adds a round-trip before each LLM call
Token budget pressure -- more examples means less room for the actual task context

agents.name: Task Agent
agents.description: The primary LLM agent that receives a prompt enriched with dynamically selected few-shot examples and performs the task

agents.name: Example Retriever
agents.description: Indexes and retrieves relevant examples from the bank based on the incoming task's characteristics -- using embedding similarity, metadata matching, or learned selection

agents.name: Bank Curator
agents.description: Maintains the example bank -- adds successful interactions, removes stale examples, verifies quality, and optimizes the index

shared: Example bank -- curated collection of input-output pairs indexed by task type, domain, and embedding
Retrieval index -- embedding-based or metadata-based index for fast example lookup
private: Task context -- the current task's input and working state
Curator logs -- records of example additions, removals, and quality assessments
memoryArchitecture.description: The example bank is the central shared resource. The retriever queries it per-request and injects matched examples into the prompt. The curator maintains bank quality over time. The task agent is unaware of the retrieval mechanism -- it simply receives a prompt with examples and executes. The bank is versioned so that example set changes can be correlated with performance changes.

diagram:
graph TD
  IN[New Task Input] --> ER[Example Retriever]
  ER -->|similarity search| EB[(Example Bank\nCurated Input-Output Pairs)]
  EB -->|top-k examples| ER
  ER -->|enriched prompt| TA[Task Agent\nLLM]
  TA --> OUT[Task Output]
  BC[Bank Curator] -->|add/remove/update| EB
  FEEDBACK[Performance Feedback] --> BC
  OUT -->|successful examples| BC

name: Prompt Compiler
summary: A meta-system compiles high-level task descriptions into optimized prompts through bootstrapping and optimization.
description: Instead of writing prompts by hand, a compilation pipeline transforms high-level task declarations (input/output signatures, constraints, metrics) into optimized prompts. The compiler can use bootstrapping (generate examples from a training set by running a teacher model), optimization (hill-climb on prompt variants using a scoring metric), or distillation (learn from a stronger model's outputs to create prompts for a weaker model). The compiled prompt includes an optimized instruction, selected few-shot examples, and formatting directives -- all discovered automatically rather than hand-tuned. This pattern exists because manual prompt engineering is brittle, non-transferable, and does not scale. Compilation treats prompts as a compilation target, not a human-written artifact.
realWorldExample: DSPy compiles declarative Python signatures (e.g., 'question -> answer') into optimized prompts through bootstrapping few-shot examples and optimizing instructions. It achieves better results than hand-written prompts by systematically searching the prompt space. TextGrad treats LLM calls as differentiable operations and optimizes prompts via textual gradients -- backpropagating natural language feedback through a computation graph. OPRO (Google DeepMind) uses an LLM to iteratively propose and evaluate its own prompt improvements, converging on high-performing instructions without human intervention.
whenToUse: When you have a measurable quality metric and want to optimize prompts systematically rather than through trial and error. When prompt quality is critical to system performance and hand-tuning has plateaued. When you need to adapt prompts to new models or domains without manual rewriting. Not needed for one-off prompts or tasks where any reasonable prompt works.
strengths: Systematic optimization outperforms manual prompt engineering on measurable metrics
Model-agnostic -- compiled prompts can be re-optimized for new models automatically
Reproducible -- the compilation process is deterministic given the same inputs and seed
Scales to complex tasks -- optimization can discover prompt structures humans would not try
weaknesses: Requires a training set and scoring metric -- not applicable to tasks without clear evaluation criteria
Compilation is expensive -- bootstrapping and optimization require many LLM calls
Opaque results -- optimized prompts may be effective but not human-readable or interpretable
Fragile to distribution shift -- prompts optimized on one data distribution may degrade on new inputs

agents.name: Prompt Compiler
agents.description: Takes high-level task declarations and produces optimized prompts through bootstrapping, optimization, and distillation. Runs the compilation pipeline end-to-end

agents.name: Teacher Model
agents.description: A stronger or more expensive LLM used to generate gold examples during bootstrapping. Its outputs become the training signal for prompt optimization

agents.name: Evaluation Agent
agents.description: Scores candidate prompts against the training set using the defined metric. Provides the optimization signal that guides the compiler toward better prompts

agents.name: Student Model
agents.description: The target LLM that will use the compiled prompt in production. May be cheaper or faster than the teacher model

shared: Task declaration -- the high-level specification of what the prompt should accomplish
Training set -- input-output examples used for bootstrapping and evaluation
Compiled prompts -- versioned optimized prompts with their evaluation scores
private: Optimization state -- the compiler's current search position, candidate set, and scoring history
memoryArchitecture.description: The compilation pipeline flows: task declaration -> bootstrapping (teacher generates examples) -> optimization (hill-climb on prompt variants) -> evaluation (score against training set) -> compiled prompt. The compiled prompt is the output artifact. Multiple compilation runs are stored and versioned so that regressions can be detected and rolled back.

diagram:
graph TD
  DECL[Task Declaration\nSignature + Metric] --> COMP[Prompt Compiler]
  COMP -->|generate examples| TM[Teacher Model\nStrong LLM]
  TM -->|gold examples| COMP
  COMP -->|candidate prompt| SM[Student Model\nTarget LLM]
  SM -->|outputs| EVAL[Evaluator\nScore Against Metric]
  EVAL -->|score| COMP
  COMP -->|iterate| COMP
  COMP -->|converged| OUT[(Compiled Prompt\nInstruction + Examples + Format)]
  TS[(Training Set)] -.-> COMP
  TS -.-> EVAL
  LOG[(Optimization Log\nVersioned Prompts + Scores)] -.-> COMP

name: Pair-Programming Copilot
summary: An agent watches developer actions in real-time and provides inline suggestions, completions, and corrections reactively.
description: An AI agent observes the developer's real-time actions -- keystrokes, file edits, cursor position, terminal output, open files -- and provides inline suggestions, code completions, and corrections without being explicitly invoked. The agent is reactive, not proactive: it responds to the developer's current intent rather than driving the task. The human maintains full control, accepting or rejecting each suggestion with a single keystroke. This is the dominant mode of AI-assisted programming -- not autonomous agents that take over, but real-time suggestion engines that amplify human intent. The pattern's power comes from its low friction: the cost of trying a suggestion is one keypress, and the cost of rejecting it is zero.
realWorldExample: GitHub Copilot's inline completion mode is the canonical example -- it watches the developer's code and context, predicts likely continuations, and shows ghost text that can be accepted with Tab. Cursor extends this with cmd-K editing (natural language to code transformations) and multi-file awareness. Supermaven optimizes for speed with a 300K-token context window and sub-100ms latency. The pattern is the single most widely adopted form of AI-assisted coding, with millions of daily active users across these tools.
whenToUse: When you want to accelerate developer productivity without changing their workflow. When the human should remain in full control of the codebase. When low-latency suggestions matter more than deep autonomous reasoning. When the task is well-defined enough that inline completions are useful. Not appropriate for complex multi-file refactoring or greenfield architecture decisions that require autonomous planning.
strengths: Zero-friction interaction -- accepting a suggestion costs one keypress, rejecting costs zero
Human retains full control -- every suggestion is explicitly accepted or ignored
Real-time feedback loop -- the agent improves suggestions based on what the developer accepts
Amplifies intent -- the developer thinks at a higher level while the agent handles boilerplate
weaknesses: Shallow reasoning -- inline suggestions optimize for likely continuations, not correctness
Context limitations -- the agent sees code around the cursor but may miss broader architectural context
Habituation risk -- developers may accept suggestions without review, introducing subtle bugs
Latency sensitivity -- suggestions must arrive within 100-300ms to feel like assistance rather than interruption

agents.name: Copilot Agent
agents.description: Monitors the developer's real-time actions (keystrokes, cursor position, file context) and generates inline suggestions, completions, and transformations

agents.name: Developer (Human)
agents.description: The human developer who writes code, accepts or rejects suggestions, and drives the overall direction. Maintains full authority over the codebase

agents.name: Context Engine
agents.description: Gathers relevant context from open files, recent edits, imports, and the broader codebase to inform the copilot's suggestions

shared: Codebase context -- the current file, open files, recent edits, and project structure
Acceptance history -- which suggestions the developer accepted, used to improve future predictions
private: Developer intent -- the human's mental model and goals, communicated only through code actions
Model cache -- the copilot's internal state for fast inference on the current context
memoryArchitecture.description: The context engine continuously updates a view of the developer's working environment. The copilot reads this context and generates suggestions. The developer's acceptance or rejection is the only feedback signal. There is no shared planning state -- the developer plans, the copilot reacts. This asymmetry is intentional: the human leads, the agent follows.

diagram:
graph LR
  DEV[Developer\nKeystrokes + Edits] --> CE[Context Engine\nOpen Files\nRecent Edits\nImports]
  CE --> COP[Copilot Agent\nLLM Inference]
  COP -->|ghost text suggestion| DEV
  DEV -->|Tab = accept| CODE[Codebase]
  DEV -->|ignore = reject| COP
  CODE -.->|updated context| CE
  HIST[(Acceptance History)] -.-> COP

name: Approval-Gate Pipeline
summary: An autonomous agent pipeline with explicit human approval gates at critical phase transitions.
description: An agent works autonomously within defined phases but must receive explicit human approval before crossing phase boundaries. Gates are inserted at high-stakes transitions: plan-to-execute, execute-to-deploy, deploy-to-rollback. The agent presents its work and proposed next steps at each gate, and the human can approve, reject, or modify. Gates can be configured by risk level -- low-risk actions proceed automatically, medium-risk actions notify but do not block, high-risk actions require explicit sign-off. This pattern exists because fully autonomous agents are not trusted for high-stakes actions, but fully manual approval of every step is too slow. Gates provide the right level of human oversight at the right moments.
realWorldExample: Replit Agent's plan-mode shows the proposed implementation plan and requires human approval before code generation begins. Devin's PR checkpoint requires human review before changes are merged. GitHub Copilot's agent mode presents a plan and waits for user confirmation before executing. Claude Code's permission system gates tool calls by category -- file writes and bash commands require explicit approval while read operations proceed automatically. The pattern generalizes to any CI/CD pipeline with manual approval stages.
whenToUse: When agents perform high-stakes actions (deploys, financial transactions, data mutations, external API calls) that require human judgment. When you want autonomous speed within phases but human oversight at transitions. When regulatory or compliance requirements mandate human-in-the-loop for certain actions. Not needed for low-stakes, easily reversible tasks.
strengths: Balances autonomy and oversight -- agents work fast within phases, humans verify at transitions
Risk-proportional -- gate strictness can be configured per action type or risk level
Auditability -- every gate approval is logged with who approved what and when
Reversible -- rejected gates let humans redirect work before irreversible actions occur
weaknesses: Human bottleneck -- gates block progress until a human responds, which may take hours or days
Approval fatigue -- too many gates lead to rubber-stamping, negating the safety benefit
Context loss -- the human reviewer may not have enough context to make a good approval decision
Configuration complexity -- defining the right gate placement and risk levels requires careful design

agents.name: Autonomous Agent
agents.description: Works freely within defined phases -- planning, implementing, testing. Pauses at phase boundaries and presents work for human review

agents.name: Gate Controller
agents.description: Manages the approval workflow. Presents the agent's work to the human, collects approval/rejection, and either unblocks the next phase or returns the agent to the current phase with feedback

agents.name: Human Reviewer
agents.description: Reviews agent work at gates and decides whether to approve, reject, or modify. Provides feedback that the agent incorporates before proceeding

shared: Approval queue -- pending gate approvals with agent work summaries and proposed next steps
Audit log -- timestamped record of all gate decisions (approved, rejected, modified) with reviewer identity
private: Agent workspace -- the autonomous agent's working state within the current phase
Reviewer context -- the human's domain knowledge and risk assessment criteria
memoryArchitecture.description: The agent works in its private workspace within each phase. At phase boundaries, it writes a summary to the approval queue. The gate controller notifies the human reviewer. The reviewer's decision (approve, reject, modify) flows back through the gate controller to the agent. The audit log captures every decision for compliance. The agent never proceeds past a gate without explicit approval.

diagram:
graph TD
  PLAN[Plan Phase\nAutonomous] --> G1{Approval Gate\nPlan Review}
  G1 -->|approved| EXEC[Execute Phase\nAutonomous]
  G1 -->|rejected + feedback| PLAN
  EXEC --> G2{Approval Gate\nCode Review}
  G2 -->|approved| DEPLOY[Deploy Phase\nAutonomous]
  G2 -->|rejected + feedback| EXEC
  DEPLOY --> G3{Approval Gate\nDeploy Review}
  G3 -->|approved| PROD[Production]
  G3 -->|rejected| ROLLBACK[Rollback]
  HR[Human Reviewer] -.->|approve/reject| G1
  HR -.->|approve/reject| G2
  HR -.->|approve/reject| G3
  LOG[(Audit Log)] -.-> G1
  LOG -.-> G2
  LOG -.-> G3

name: Prompt Cache Optimization
summary: Agents structure prompts so static prefixes are cached by the LLM provider, cutting cost and latency for repeated calls.
description: Agents deliberately structure their prompts so that the static prefix -- system prompt, tool definitions, few-shot examples, reference documents -- stays identical across calls, enabling the LLM provider's prompt caching to avoid re-processing those tokens. Variable content (conversation history, current query) is appended at the end where it does not invalidate the cache. This requires deliberate prompt architecture: system instructions first, tool schemas second, reference context third, conversation last. The cost savings are substantial (up to 90% on cached prefixes) and the latency reduction is significant because cached tokens skip the prefill stage. The pattern is invisible in the API response but requires careful prompt ordering to activate.
realWorldExample: Anthropic's prompt caching gives a 90% cost reduction and faster time-to-first-token on cached prefixes for Claude. Google's context caching for Gemini provides similar savings for repeated long contexts. The pattern requires deliberate prompt architecture -- system prompt and tool definitions first, conversation history last. Google ADK's context engineering documentation explicitly designs prompts for prefix caching. Production agent systems that make many calls with the same tools and instructions see dramatic cost reductions by ordering their prompts to maximize prefix overlap.
whenToUse: When your agent makes many LLM calls with the same system prompt, tool definitions, or reference context. When cost or latency is a concern and your prompt has a large static prefix. When building agent systems that process many requests with identical configurations. Not beneficial for one-off requests or prompts where the entire content changes between calls.
strengths: Up to 90% cost reduction on cached prefix tokens -- significant for large system prompts
Faster time-to-first-token -- cached tokens skip the prefill computation stage
Zero code changes to the agent logic -- only prompt ordering needs to change
Compounds with other optimizations -- caching + smaller models + batching multiply savings
weaknesses: Requires careful prompt ordering -- any change to the prefix invalidates the cache
Provider-specific -- caching behavior varies between Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google
Invisible feedback -- there is no way to verify cache hits without provider-side metrics
Fragile to dynamic prefixes -- if any part of the system prompt changes per-request, caching breaks

agents.name: Prompt Architect
agents.description: Designs and maintains the prompt structure to maximize prefix stability. Ensures system prompt, tool definitions, and reference context are front-loaded and invariant across calls

agents.name: LLM Agent
agents.description: The agent that makes LLM calls with the cache-optimized prompt structure. Appends variable content (conversation, current query) after the stable prefix

agents.name: Cost Monitor
agents.description: Tracks token usage, cache hit rates, and cost per call to verify that the caching strategy is working and identify regressions

shared: Prompt template -- the structured prompt with stable prefix and variable suffix clearly delineated
Cost metrics -- per-call token usage, cache hit rates, and cost tracking
private: Conversation state -- the variable suffix that changes per-call (conversation history, current query)
memoryArchitecture.description: The prompt is divided into a stable prefix (system prompt, tool schemas, reference docs) and a variable suffix (conversation history, current query). The prefix is identical across calls and triggers the provider's cache. The suffix changes per-call. Cost monitoring verifies that caching is working by tracking token usage patterns. The prompt architect maintains the template and ensures no per-request variability leaks into the prefix.

diagram:
graph TD
  subgraph Stable Prefix -- Cached
    SYS[System Prompt] --> TOOLS[Tool Definitions]
    TOOLS --> REFS[Reference Documents]
    REFS --> FEW[Few-Shot Examples]
  end
  subgraph Variable Suffix -- Not Cached
    CONV[Conversation History]
    QUERY[Current Query]
  end
  FEW --> CONV
  CONV --> QUERY
  QUERY --> LLM[LLM API Call]
  LLM -->|cached prefix: 90% cost reduction| RESP[Response]
  CM[Cost Monitor] -.->|track cache hits| LLM

name: Semantic Router
summary: A lightweight embedding model routes requests by comparing input against example utterances, orders of magnitude faster than LLM routing.
description: Instead of using a full LLM to classify and route user requests (slow, expensive), a lightweight embedding model encodes the input and compares it against a bank of example utterances for each possible route. The closest match by cosine similarity determines routing. Each route has a set of representative utterances that define its semantic boundary. New routes are added by providing examples, not by rewriting routing logic. This pattern is orders of magnitude faster (milliseconds vs. seconds) and cheaper (embedding vs. LLM inference) than LLM-based routing, making it suitable as a first-pass classifier before invoking expensive specialist agents. The trade-off is lower accuracy on ambiguous or novel inputs that do not match any route's examples well.
realWorldExample: Aurelio AI's semantic-router library provides sub-millisecond intent classification using embedding similarity against predefined routes with example utterances. AWS Bedrock's intent classification uses a similar embedding-based approach for routing to specialized agents. Many production chatbot systems use embedding-based routing as a first pass before invoking expensive LLM agents -- if the route is confident, skip the LLM classification entirely. The pattern is also used in RAG systems to route queries to the correct knowledge base before retrieval.
whenToUse: When you need to route requests to different agents or handlers and latency or cost makes LLM-based classification impractical. When your route categories are well-defined with clear example utterances. When you need a fast first-pass classifier that can be followed by a more expensive LLM classifier for ambiguous cases. Not appropriate when routing requires complex reasoning about the request content.
strengths: Millisecond latency -- embedding comparison is orders of magnitude faster than LLM inference
Minimal cost -- embedding API calls are 10-100x cheaper than LLM calls
Easy to extend -- new routes are added by providing example utterances, no code changes needed
Deterministic -- the same input always routes to the same destination (no temperature variance)
weaknesses: Limited to semantic similarity -- cannot reason about complex or ambiguous routing criteria
Example quality is critical -- poorly chosen utterances create misroutes
No explanation -- unlike LLM routing, semantic routing cannot explain why it chose a route
Edge cases fall through -- novel inputs that do not match any route's examples are misclassified

agents.name: Semantic Router
agents.description: Encodes the input with a lightweight embedding model, compares against route exemplars via cosine similarity, and dispatches to the highest-scoring route

agents.name: Specialist Agent
agents.description: A domain-specific agent that handles requests routed to its category. Each specialist handles one route

agents.name: Fallback Agent
agents.description: Handles requests where no route scores above the confidence threshold. May use a full LLM to classify ambiguous inputs or ask the user for clarification

shared: Route registry -- each route's name, example utterances, and pre-computed embeddings
Routing log -- record of classifications with confidence scores for monitoring and tuning
private: Specialist context -- each specialist agent's domain-specific system prompt and tool set
Embedding model -- the lightweight model used for encoding, shared by the router
memoryArchitecture.description: The route registry is the central configuration. Each route has a set of example utterances with pre-computed embeddings. The router encodes the incoming input, computes cosine similarity against all route embeddings, and dispatches to the highest-scoring route. The routing log enables monitoring of classification quality and identification of routes that need more or better examples.

diagram:
graph TD
  IN[User Request] --> ENC[Encode Input\nEmbedding Model]
  ENC --> CMP[Compare Against\nRoute Exemplars]
  R1[(Route: Billing\nExemplar Embeddings)] -.-> CMP
  R2[(Route: Technical\nExemplar Embeddings)] -.-> CMP
  R3[(Route: Sales\nExemplar Embeddings)] -.-> CMP
  CMP -->|highest similarity| DISP{Confidence\nAbove Threshold?}
  DISP -->|yes: billing| S1[Billing Agent]
  DISP -->|yes: technical| S2[Technical Agent]
  DISP -->|yes: sales| S3[Sales Agent]
  DISP -->|no: ambiguous| FB[Fallback Agent\nFull LLM Classification]

name: Multi-Model Cascade
summary: Cheap models handle easy requests; uncertain or complex ones escalate to more capable models through confidence thresholds.
description: A cascade of models ordered by cost and capability. The cheapest, fastest model handles every request first. If it detects uncertainty (low confidence score, hedging language, explicit 'I don't know') or complexity (multi-step reasoning required, domain-specific knowledge), it escalates to the next model in the cascade. Each level has a confidence threshold that triggers escalation. The cascade can have multiple levels: a tiny classifier, a fast small model, a medium model, and a frontier model as the final fallback. Most requests are handled cheaply at the first level; only genuinely hard requests reach the expensive models. This pattern optimizes the cost-quality tradeoff by matching request difficulty to model capability.
realWorldExample: Martian's Model Router dynamically selects between models based on predicted task complexity, routing easy tasks to cheap models and hard tasks to expensive ones. OpenRouter provides model routing across providers with cost and quality optimization. Many production systems use Haiku or Flash for initial classification and routing, then escalate to Opus or GPT-4 for complex reasoning. Anthropic's own API documentation suggests this pattern for cost optimization -- use Claude Haiku for simple tasks, Claude Sonnet for medium tasks, and Claude Opus for the hardest tasks.
whenToUse: When your request distribution has a mix of easy and hard tasks and you want to optimize cost without sacrificing quality on hard tasks. When latency matters for easy tasks but quality matters for hard ones. When you can define confidence thresholds or complexity heuristics that reliably distinguish easy from hard requests. Not needed if all requests are equally complex or if cost is not a concern.
strengths: Dramatic cost reduction -- most requests are handled by cheap models, expensive models only handle the tail
Maintains quality ceiling -- the hardest requests still reach the most capable model
Latency improvement -- easy requests get fast responses from small models
Graceful degradation -- if the frontier model is unavailable, the cascade still handles easy requests
weaknesses: Threshold tuning is critical -- too aggressive escalation wastes money, too conservative misses quality issues
Cascade latency on hard tasks -- escalating through multiple levels adds round-trip time
Confidence calibration -- models are not always well-calibrated in their uncertainty estimates
Complexity in evaluation -- you need metrics to verify that the cascade produces equivalent quality to always using the best model

agents.name: Fast Model (Tier 1)
agents.description: The cheapest, fastest model. Handles every request first. Produces a response with a confidence signal. If confident, the response is returned directly; if uncertain, it escalates

agents.name: Medium Model (Tier 2)
agents.description: A more capable model that handles requests escalated from Tier 1. Provides higher quality at higher cost. Can further escalate to Tier 3 if it detects the task exceeds its capabilities

agents.name: Frontier Model (Tier 3)
agents.description: The most capable and expensive model. Handles the hardest requests that lower tiers cannot resolve with confidence. The final fallback -- its response is always returned

agents.name: Cascade Controller
agents.description: Routes requests through the cascade, evaluates confidence signals, decides whether to escalate, and tracks cost and quality metrics

shared: Cascade configuration -- model ordering, confidence thresholds, and escalation rules
Metrics log -- per-request records of which model tier handled it, cost, latency, and confidence scores
private: Model context -- each model's ephemeral context for the current request
memoryArchitecture.description: The cascade controller manages the flow. Each model is stateless -- it receives the request, produces a response with a confidence signal, and the controller decides whether to return the response or escalate. The metrics log enables threshold tuning: if Tier 1 is escalating too much, its threshold may be too conservative; if Tier 3 is producing lower quality than expected, the escalation criteria may be wrong.

diagram:
graph TD
  REQ[User Request] --> CC[Cascade Controller]
  CC --> T1[Tier 1: Fast Model\nHaiku / Flash]
  T1 -->|confident: 85%+| RESP[Response]
  T1 -->|uncertain| CC
  CC --> T2[Tier 2: Medium Model\nSonnet / GPT-4o]
  T2 -->|confident: 80%+| RESP
  T2 -->|uncertain| CC
  CC --> T3[Tier 3: Frontier Model\nOpus / GPT-4]
  T3 -->|always returns| RESP
  METRICS[(Metrics Log\nCost + Latency + Tier)] -.-> CC

name: Agentic RAG
summary: An agent decides when to retrieve, what to search for, and whether results are sufficient, rather than always retrieving.
description: Unlike basic RAG (retrieve then generate), agentic RAG puts the agent in control of the retrieval process. The agent decides WHEN to retrieve (not every query needs retrieval), WHAT to search for (reformulating queries for better results), WHERE to search (selecting among multiple knowledge bases or search engines), and WHETHER the results are sufficient (triggering re-retrieval with refined queries if quality is low). The retrieval is agent-directed rather than automatic -- it is a tool the agent uses strategically, not a fixed preprocessing step. This enables multi-hop reasoning where the agent retrieves, reasons about the results, retrieves again with refined queries, and iterates until it has enough context. The key difference from basic RAG is the feedback loop: the agent evaluates retrieval quality and adapts its strategy.
realWorldExample: LlamaIndex's agentic RAG lets agents decide retrieval strategy dynamically -- choosing between vector search, keyword search, SQL queries, and API calls based on the question type. Perplexity AI's search agent reformulates queries, evaluates source quality, and performs multi-hop retrieval across the web. Anthropic's Claude can use search tools selectively rather than always retrieving -- it decides whether a question needs retrieval or can be answered from its training data. Corrective RAG (CRAG) adds a grading step that evaluates retrieved document relevance and triggers web search as a fallback when retrieval quality is low.
whenToUse: When queries vary in their retrieval needs -- some need deep search, some need none. When you have multiple knowledge sources and the right source depends on the query. When basic RAG fails because the initial retrieval misses relevant documents. When you need multi-hop reasoning that requires iterative retrieval and reasoning. Not needed for simple Q&A over a single well-indexed knowledge base.
strengths: Adaptive retrieval -- the agent retrieves only when needed and from the right sources
Query reformulation improves retrieval quality compared to using the raw user query
Multi-hop reasoning -- the agent can chain multiple retrievals to answer complex questions
Self-correcting -- the agent detects low-quality retrieval and retries with better queries
weaknesses: Higher latency -- agent-directed retrieval adds decision overhead compared to automatic retrieval
Retrieval loops -- the agent may over-retrieve, searching repeatedly without converging on an answer
Tool call overhead -- each retrieval decision requires an LLM call to evaluate whether to retrieve
Harder to evaluate -- the agent's retrieval decisions add a dimension of variability beyond the generation

agents.name: Reasoning Agent
agents.description: The primary LLM agent that decides when, what, and where to retrieve. Evaluates retrieval results and decides whether to answer, re-retrieve, or reformulate the query

agents.name: Retrieval Tool
agents.description: Executes search queries against knowledge bases, vector stores, web search, or APIs. Returns ranked documents with relevance scores

agents.name: Relevance Grader
agents.description: Evaluates whether retrieved documents are relevant and sufficient for answering the query. Triggers re-retrieval or query reformulation if quality is low

shared: Knowledge bases -- vector stores, document indexes, web search APIs available for retrieval
Retrieval log -- record of queries, retrieved documents, relevance scores, and reformulation history
private: Agent reasoning state -- the agent's current understanding and what information gaps remain
Query reformulation history -- past queries and their results, used to avoid repeating failed searches
memoryArchitecture.description: The reasoning agent maintains an internal model of what it knows and what it needs. It formulates queries, invokes retrieval tools, evaluates results with the grader, and iterates. The knowledge bases are external shared resources. The retrieval log provides observability into the agent's search strategy. The key difference from basic RAG is that the agent controls the loop -- retrieval is not a fixed step but a strategic tool.

diagram:
graph TD
  Q[User Query] --> RA[Reasoning Agent]
  RA -->|need retrieval?| DEC{Decision}
  DEC -->|no: answer from knowledge| ANS[Answer]
  DEC -->|yes: formulate query| RET[Retrieval Tool]
  RET -->|search| KB1[(Vector Store)]
  RET -->|search| KB2[(Web Search)]
  RET -->|search| KB3[(SQL Database)]
  RET -->|documents| GR[Relevance Grader]
  GR -->|sufficient| RA
  GR -->|insufficient| REF[Reformulate Query]
  REF --> RET
  RA -->|enough context| ANS

name: Code Generation with Execution Feedback
summary: The agent generates code, executes it in a sandbox, reads the output or errors, and iterates until correctness.
description: The agent generates code, sends it to an execution environment (sandbox, REPL, notebook), receives the actual output or error messages, and uses that ground-truth feedback to iterate. The execution environment provides deterministic feedback that the agent cannot hallucinate -- a TypeError is a TypeError, a plot is a plot, a test failure shows the exact assertion that failed. This tight generate-execute-debug loop is fundamentally different from generate-only patterns because the feedback is not another LLM's opinion but actual program behavior. The agent learns from concrete execution results rather than speculative reasoning about what the code might do. This pattern dominates data science, exploratory programming, and test-driven agent development.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's Code Interpreter executes Python in a sandboxed container and returns outputs including dataframes, plots, and error messages that the agent uses to refine its code. Claude's analysis tool provides the same loop -- write Python, see output, iterate. Jupyter AI and Google Colab AI integrate LLM code generation directly into notebook execution environments. The pattern is the foundation of every coding agent that runs code: Replit Agent, Devin, Cursor's terminal integration, and Claude Code all implement variants of this generate-execute-debug cycle.
whenToUse: When the agent needs to produce correct code and execution provides a reliable feedback signal. When tasks involve data analysis, computation, or any work where running the code reveals whether it is right. When speculative reasoning about code correctness is unreliable. Not appropriate for code that cannot be safely executed (production database mutations, irreversible API calls) without additional safeguards.
strengths: Ground-truth feedback -- execution results are deterministic and cannot be hallucinated
Self-correcting -- error messages give the agent concrete information to fix its code
Exploratory programming -- the agent can try approaches and see what works rather than planning perfectly upfront
Supports complex outputs -- plots, dataframes, and rich outputs are verified visually, not just textually
weaknesses: Execution latency -- each iteration requires running the code, which adds seconds per cycle
Sandbox limitations -- the execution environment may not have all required libraries or data
Infinite loop risk -- the agent can get stuck in a generate-fail-retry cycle without making progress
Security requires sandboxing -- executing agent-generated code without isolation is dangerous

agents.name: Code Agent
agents.description: Generates code based on the task and execution feedback. Reads error messages and outputs to refine its approach. Iterates until the code produces correct results

agents.name: Execution Environment
agents.description: Runs generated code in a sandboxed environment (container, REPL, notebook). Returns stdout, stderr, exit codes, and rich outputs (plots, dataframes). Provides the ground-truth feedback loop

agents.name: Output Validator
agents.description: Evaluates execution results against the task requirements. Determines whether the output is correct, partially correct, or needs another iteration. May run test assertions

shared: Execution history -- generated code, outputs, and errors from each iteration
Working files -- data files, generated artifacts, and intermediate results in the execution environment
private: Agent reasoning -- the code agent's understanding of the task and its evolving approach
Environment state -- the executor's runtime state (loaded libraries, variables, file system)
memoryArchitecture.description: The execution environment maintains state across iterations within a session -- variables, loaded data, and file system persist so the agent can build incrementally. The execution history gives the agent concrete feedback on what worked and what failed. The validator decides when the loop terminates. The key architectural property is that feedback is deterministic: the same code always produces the same output.

diagram:
graph TD
  TASK[Task Description] --> CA[Code Agent\nLLM]
  CA -->|generated code| EX[Execution Environment\nSandbox / REPL]
  EX -->|stdout + stderr| CA
  EX -->|plots + dataframes| CA
  EX -->|error messages| CA
  CA -->|refined code| EX
  EX -->|results| VAL[Output Validator]
  VAL -->|correct| OUT[Final Output]
  VAL -->|needs work| CA
  HIST[(Execution History\nCode + Outputs)] -.-> CA
